finding.title: Session logs, screen snapshots, and metadata created world/group-readable (0644 files in 0755 dirs), exposing every session secret to other local users
finding.detail: The code locks down only the socket (chmod 0600, run_daemon src/main.rs:343) and the FIFO (mkfifo 0600, src/main.rs:644); everything else is created under the default umask: ~/.agent-bridge and every session dir via create_dir_all (0755), raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt via File::create (0666 & umask = 0644), metadata.json via fs::write (0644). Verified empirically on this machine (umask 022 -> dirs 0755, files 0644). raw.log/clean.log capture every PTY byte — typed prompts, pasted keys, terminal-echoed input, and all program output (API keys, auth tokens, source, Claude transcripts) — and metadata.json records the full command line, which may carry secrets as arguments. On stock macOS every human account is in group staff and /Users/<user> is drwxr-x--- (group staff r-x), so another local staff user can traverse home -> ~/.agent-bridge (0755) -> sessions/<name> (0755) and read all transcripts; on any host with a 0755 home, 'other' gets in too. No chmod is ever applied to these artifacts, so the exposure lasts their whole lifetime.
finding.anchor: initialize_session_files src/main.rs:647 (also run_daemon create_dir_all src/main.rs:330, start_session_detached src/main.rs:534, save_metadata src/main.rs:1188)
finding.fix: Create the bridge root and session dirs with mode 0700 (DirBuilderExt, or set umask(0077) at daemon/supervisor startup) and open all logs/metadata/screen files with OpenOptions::mode(0o600); chmod existing artifacts on open as defense-in-depth.

verdict.reasoning: Every element of the claim checks out against the code and against stock macOS semantics. Code: the only permission calls in src/main.rs are the socket chmod 0600 (line 343) and mkfifo 0600 (line 644); grep confirms no umask(), no other set_permissions, no OpenOptionsExt::mode anywhere. The bridge root, sessions root, and per-session dirs are made with fs::create_dir_all (lines 330, 534, 592, 992) = mode 0777 & ~umask = 0755; raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt are made with File::create (lines 647-649) = 0666 & ~umask = 0644; metadata.json via fs::write (line 1188) = 0644; the append re-opens (769-778) and fs::write rewrites (801) preserve those modes. An empirical probe on this machine (umask 022, the macOS default) reproduced exactly 0755 dirs / 0644 files. Content sensitivity is real: capture_output writes every PTY byte to raw.log/clean.log, which includes terminal-echoed input sent via send/send-keys plus all program output, and metadata.json stores the full command vector (lines 654, 739). Traversal path verified on this stock macOS 26.5.1 host: /Users is 755; /Users/chadpeppers is drwxr-x--- chadpeppers:staff (the only ACL is group:everyone deny delete, which does not block reads); every local macOS account has primary gid 20 (staff), so any other local account can traverse home -> ~/.agent-bridge (0755) -> sessions/<name> (0755) and read the 0644 logs/metadata for their entire lifetime; ~/.agent-bridge is not TCC-protected. Refutation angles eliminated: home dir is not 0700 on stock macOS (group staff gets r-x; older 755-home systems expose to 'other' as well), umask is not restrictive, and no code path ever chmods the artifacts. Severity downgraded from critical to high: the control plane is correctly locked down (FIFO 0600, socket 0600), so other users cannot inject input or drive sessions - the exposure is read-only disclosure, which is the severity guide's high bullet verbatim ('secrets exposed beyond the owning user'), and it requires a second local account to exist (this particular machine currently has only one).
verdict.fix: Set umask(0o077) once at the top of main() (unsafe { libc::umask(0o077); }) so every creation site inherits owner-only modes; additionally (defense in depth) create directories with DirBuilder::new().recursive(true).mode(0o700) at src/main.rs:330, 534, 592, 992, and open raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt/metadata.json with OpenOptions::new().write(true).create(true).mode(0o600) instead of File::create/fs::write at lines 647-649, 801, 1188 (the truncating rewrites preserve the inode mode, so fixing creation modes covers steady state). On session start, chmod any pre-existing session dir/files to 0700/0600 to repair artifacts created by older versions.

finding.title: metadata.json written with truncate-then-write (fs::write), racing every concurrent reader and writer with no atomicity
finding.detail: save_metadata uses fs::write — open(O_TRUNC) then write, two syscalls, no atomicity — while concurrent readers and writers exist by design with zero locks. (a) Every direct-mode start polls load_metadata every 100ms (wait_for_running_metadata, line 668) while the supervisor writes the file 2-3 times (lines 692, 743); a read landing in the truncate window gets empty/partial JSON and the `?` at line 668 aborts start with 'failed to parse metadata.json' even though the session started fine (the manager may then retry, feeding the duplicate-start race); the same transient failure hits send/status (lines 872, 961), and list/shutdown silently drop the session from results (lines 1002, 1045). (b) Every stop of a supervisor session has TWO concurrent mark_stopped writers — the stopper (line 1099, 'stopped by user') and the exiting supervisor (line 695, real exit status) — racing unsynchronized load-modify-write truncate+write pairs from independent fds: last writer wins, and interleaving can leave mixed-version, unparseable JSON persistently on disk. (c) stop SIGKILLs the supervisor (line 1090) possibly inside mark_stopped, and daemon Shutdown calls process::exit(0) while other threads may be mid-save_metadata; either can cut a write between truncate and write, leaving metadata.json empty/truncated forever — after which status/send/stop all fail for that session (and it vanishes from list) until it is re-started.
finding.fix: Write to a temp file in the same directory and atomically rename() over metadata.json (fixes torn reads and interleaved writers); make pollers tolerate one transient parse failure, and order the stop-vs-supervisor writers (e.g. supervisor only writes if still Running).

verdict.reasoning: Core claim verified against the code. save_metadata (src/main.rs:1188) uses fs::write, which is File::create (open O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC) followed by a separate write(2) — non-atomic, with an empty-file window between the two syscalls (microseconds, up to ~ms if the writer is preempted between them). There is zero synchronization in the codebase (no flock/fcntl/rename). Concurrent cross-process readers exist by design: wait_for_running_metadata (line 668) polls load_metadata every 100ms with `?` while the supervisor process saves at lines 692 and 743; an empty read makes serde_json fail ("EOF while parsing a value") and start aborts with "failed to parse metadata.json" even though the detached supervisor keeps running. The same `?` hits send (line 872) and status (line 961); list (1002) and daemon shutdown (1045) silently skip the session. Claim (c) is also real: terminate_pid(supervisor) at line 1090 can fatally signal the supervisor while it is inside mark_stopped (line 695, between reaping the child and completing the save), and daemon Shutdown's process::exit(0) at line 388 can land while a session thread (line 616) is mid-save_metadata — either cuts between truncate and write, leaving metadata.json persistently empty, breaking status/send/stop and dropping the session from list until a re-start rewrites it (recovery works because start_session_detached tolerates the load error at line 537). Two corrections to the claim: (1) sub-claim (b)'s interleaved mixed-version JSON is impossible in direct mode — stop_session_silent kills the supervisor (line 1090) and waits for process_alive to go false BEFORE its own mark_stopped (line 1099), and a fatally-signaled process executes no further user code, so the two saves are strictly sequential there; true concurrent dual mark_stopped writers exist only for daemon-owned sessions (the session thread is never signaled), and even then interleaving needs microsecond coincidence. (2) The "duplicate-start race" embellishment is marginal: a retry almost always finds valid Running metadata and gets a correct "already running" error. Severity downgraded to medium: all windows are microsecond-scale against 100ms pollers (~1e-4 per start for the transient abort, far rarer for persistent corruption), save_metadata runs only at start/stop transitions so steady-state send/status essentially never race, torn partial-JSON reads are excluded (single sub-page write(2), macOS vnode read/write serialization — the only observable bad state is the empty file), and recovery is a session re-start. This matches the guide's medium definition ("failure under plausible-but-less-common conditions: races, crashes leaving stale state") rather than high ("real failure in normal everyday use").
verdict.fix: In save_metadata, write atomically: serialize to a temp file in the same session directory (e.g. metadata.json.tmp.{pid}), then fs::rename() it over metadata.json. rename(2) is atomic on POSIX, so readers always see a complete old or new document, and a kill or process::exit mid-save leaves at worst a stray temp file instead of a truncated metadata.json. Optionally harden the consumers: let wait_for_running_metadata tolerate a transient load_metadata failure (retry instead of `?`), and make mark_stopped skip the write (or preserve the existing exit_status) when the loaded status is already Stopped, so the stopper does not overwrite the supervisor's real exit status.

finding.title: Daemon-routed start resolves omitted/relative --cwd against the daemon's working directory, not the client's
finding.detail: daemon_request_for_command forwards cwd: Option<PathBuf> verbatim (src/main.rs:243-247) without client-side resolution; start_session_in_daemon resolves None via std::env::current_dir() OF THE DAEMON PROCESS and canonicalizes relative paths against the daemon's cwd (src/main.rs:583-586). In the recommended setup (daemon up, commands auto-routed), `agent-bridge start s --cmd claude` run from inside a project silently starts Claude in whatever directory the daemon was launched from ($HOME, etc.), so the coding agent reads/edits the wrong repository. Verified empirically: with a daemon launched from /private/tmp/ab-daemon-cwd, `start py --cmd "python3 -i"` from ~/Projects/agent-bridge produced a session whose status reports cwd /private/tmp/ab-daemon-cwd. `--cwd ./subdir` likewise resolves against the daemon's cwd (wrong directory or a confusing 'failed to canonicalize cwd' error). Direct mode resolves the client's cwd correctly, so behavior changes silently depending on whether a daemon happens to be reachable.
finding.fix: Resolve and canonicalize cwd in the client before building DaemonRequest::Start (always send an absolute path; make the field non-optional on the wire) and have the daemon reject Start requests with missing/relative paths.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code walk. (1) Client: main (src/main.rs:203-219) auto-routes Start to the daemon whenever the socket connects and --direct is absent; daemon_request_for_command (src/main.rs:243-247) forwards cwd: Option<PathBuf> verbatim with no client-side current_dir()/canonicalize(), and DaemonRequest::Start (src/main.rs:160-164) has no field carrying the client's directory, so the daemon cannot recover it. On a daemon response, main returns at line 208 — the correct local path (start_session, line 222) never runs; fallback to local occurs only on NotFound/ConnectionRefused (lines 280-288). (2) Daemon: handle_daemon_request (line 396) calls start_session_in_daemon, whose lines 583-586 do cwd.unwrap_or(std::env::current_dir()?).canonicalize()? inside the daemon process; current_dir() is the daemon's getcwd, and Path::canonicalize is realpath(3), which resolves relative paths against the calling process's cwd — also the daemon's. run_daemon (lines 328-361) never chdirs (no set_current_dir/chdir anywhere in the file), so its cwd is wherever the user happened to launch `agent-bridge daemon`. (3) The wrong cwd takes real effect: it is recorded in metadata (line 653) and set as the PTY child's working directory via command.cwd(cwd) in supervise_pty (line 724), so the spawned agent genuinely runs there; format_started_session (lines 624-635) prints only pids, making the misdirection silent. (4) Direct mode (start_session_detached, lines 525-528) performs the identical resolution in the client process, establishing the intended "omitted --cwd = caller's directory" semantics and confirming the silent behavioral flip based on daemon reachability. `--cwd ./relative` similarly resolves against the daemon's cwd or fails with "failed to canonicalize cwd". Not an excluded roadmap item. Severity high: silent wrong behavior in the recommended everyday setup (daemon up, start from inside a project without --cwd), with the consequence that a coding agent operates on the wrong repository; not critical because no security boundary is crossed and agent-bridge itself does not corrupt data.
verdict.fix: Resolve cwd in the client before the request is sent: in main (or by changing daemon_request_for_command to return Result), for Commands::Start compute `let cwd = cwd.map_or_else(std::env::current_dir, Ok)?.canonicalize()?` and send DaemonRequest::Start with that absolute path (ideally make the wire field a non-optional PathBuf). As defense in depth, have start_session_in_daemon bail on a missing or non-absolute cwd instead of falling back to the daemon's current_dir().

finding.title: Daemon mode permanently leaks the forward_input thread, the FIFO fd, and the PTY master fd per session lifecycle, eventually wedging the daemon on EMFILE/PTY exhaustion
finding.detail: forward_input loops forever (src/main.rs:809-830): it holds the FIFO open O_RDWR — the thread is its own writer, so read() never returns EOF and the read==0 branch is dead code — and nothing signals it when the child exits; supervise_pty joins only the output thread (line 763) and abandons the input thread (`_input_thread`, line 758). The thread also owns the PTY master writer (portable-pty take_writer dups the master fd — verified in portable-pty unix.rs — so drop(pair.master) does not release the PTY). In direct mode the supervisor process exit reaps everything, but in the long-lived daemon every start/stop cycle permanently leaks 1 blocked OS thread + 2 fds (the old FIFO inode and the dup'd PTY master) + 1 system-wide PTY slot (the device frees only when the last master fd closes). At macOS's default 256-fd soft limit, roughly 120 session lifecycles wedge the daemon: openpty fails ('failed to open PTY') for all new sessions, listener.accept() degenerates into a hot error-print spin taking down all commands, and ptmx exhaustion can affect other terminal apps. Only a daemon restart recovers.
finding.anchor: forward_input src/main.rs:809 (spawned and abandoned at supervise_pty src/main.rs:758)
finding.fix: After child.wait(), set an atomic stop flag and wake the forwarder (sentinel byte into the FIFO, or switch it to poll() with a self-pipe/read timeout) so it exits and drops its FIFO and PTY-writer fds, then join it before supervise_pty returns.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by walking the full path in src/main.rs plus the pinned crate sources. (1) forward_input (main.rs:809-830) opens the FIFO read+write in blocking mode; because its own open file description is a FIFO writer, blocking read() can never return EOF (read==0 branch is dead code) and blocks forever once the FIFO is empty. No wake mechanism exists: after the child exits, write_session_bytes refuses sends (status != Running, main.rs:873-875), and a same-name restart unlinks the old FIFO and creates a new inode (initialize_session_files main.rs:641-645), so the old thread's read never completes. (2) supervise_pty detaches the input thread (handle dropped at `let _input_thread`, main.rs:758) and joins only the output thread (main.rs:763); grep confirms no stop flag or join anywhere. (3) The thread owns the PTY master writer: portable-pty 0.8.1 unix.rs:319-326 take_writer does PtyFd(self.fd.try_clone()?), and filedescriptor 0.8.3 unix.rs:149 implements try_clone as fcntl(F_DUPFD_CLOEXEC) — a real kernel dup. So drop(pair.master) (main.rs:762) does not release the PTY; the pty pair stays allocated until the leaked dup closes. (4) Direct mode is unaffected (supervisor process exit reaps everything, main.rs:686-698), but daemon mode runs supervise_pty on a thread inside the long-lived daemon (main.rs:610-619), so every start/stop cycle permanently leaks 1 blocked OS thread + 1 FIFO fd + 1 dup'd PTY master fd (holding 1 system pty slot). At macOS's default 256 soft fd limit this wedges the daemon after roughly 120-125 lifecycles: openpty fails ("failed to open PTY", main.rs:711-718) for all new sessions; accept() returns EMFILE while the pending connection stays queued (BSD semantics), so the listener loop (main.rs:347-358) spins printing errors; clients connect via the backlog and then hang forever in read_line (main.rs:296-300 has no timeout) — i.e., all auto-routed commands hang, recoverable only by restarting the daemon. The child is setsid'd by portable-pty (unix.rs:220) so stop signals never reach the daemon to clean anything up. Two minor overstatements in the claim do not change the verdict: the UnixMasterWriter Drop (EOT write) never runs since the thread never exits, and system-wide ptmx exhaustion is bounded (~125 leaked ptys vs the macOS default kern.tty.ptmx_max of 511) because the daemon's own fd limit hits first. Severity stays high: the leak is unconditional on every session lifecycle in the default (daemon-routed) mode of a tool built for agent-driven session churn, the eventual failure mode is total (all commands hang, new sessions impossible), and there is no self-recovery.
verdict.fix: In supervise_pty, keep the input thread's JoinHandle and make it exitable: share an Arc<AtomicBool> stop flag with forward_input; have forward_input check the flag after every read and return when set. After child.wait() returns, set the flag, then wake the blocked reader by opening the session FIFO O_WRONLY|O_NONBLOCK (same pattern as write_session_bytes) and writing a single sentinel byte (discarded by the forwarder when the flag is set), then join the input thread before supervise_pty returns. The thread exit drops both the FIFO fd and the UnixMasterWriter (the dup'd PTY master fd), freeing the pty slot. Alternatively, open the FIFO in forward_input with O_NONBLOCK and convert the loop to treat EAGAIN as sleep-then-check-flag, which removes the need for the sentinel write.

finding.title: stop blind-signals persisted PIDs with no identity or status check: after crash/reboot it SIGKILLs a reused PID and its whole process group, and EPERM wedges the session forever
finding.detail: stop_session_silent passes child_pid/supervisor_pid from metadata.json to terminate_pid -> signal_pid_and_group, which sends SIGTERM then SIGKILL to both kill(pid) and kill(-pid) — the entire process group — with no identity check and no status gate. Stale Running metadata is routine: daemon SIGKILL (daemon-owned sessions are never recovered on restart), supervisor crash, or reboot all skip mark_stopped. After PID reuse, a later `agent-bridge stop name` SIGKILLs an unrelated process and, via the -pid group signal, potentially an entire unrelated process group. If the recycled pid belongs to another user/root, kill returns EPERM: stop_session_silent bails BEFORE mark_stopped (src/main.rs:1095-1099) and process_alive treats EPERM as alive (src/main.rs:1311), so the session is stuck Running permanently — stop always fails, start of the same name is refused 'already running', recoverable only by hand-editing the session directory. Additionally, mark_stopped retains the pids and stop never checks status==Stopped, so stopping an already-exited session re-signals possibly-recycled pids, can report 'failed to stop session' for a session that is in fact stopped, and overwrites the real recorded exit_status with 'stopped by user'.
finding.fix: Record the child's process start time (libproc on macOS, /proc stat on Linux) in metadata and verify identity before signaling; treat stale/unsignalable targets as already gone and still mark_stopped; short-circuit when status is already Stopped and clear pids in mark_stopped; never group-kill a possibly-reused pid.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited mechanism checks out in /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) stop_session_silent (1078-1102) signals metadata.child_pid and metadata.supervisor_pid with no status gate and no identity check, and bails at 1095-1097 BEFORE mark_stopped (1099) if any signal errored. (2) signal_pid_and_group (1133-1150) sends to both Pid::from_raw(-pid) and pid — POSIX group kill via nix/libc — tolerating only ESRCH; EPERM becomes an error that propagates through terminate_pid line 1109. (3) process_alive (1308-1314) maps EPERM to alive. (4) Stale Running metadata is real: mark_stopped is only called from run_supervisor (695-696), the daemon session thread (616), and stop itself; nothing reconciles status, so supervisor crash, daemon SIGKILL, or reboot leaves status=Running permanently. (5) mark_stopped (1168-1174) retains pids, so stopping an already-Stopped session re-signals possibly-recycled pids and overwrites the recorded exit_status with "stopped by user". Failure scenarios confirmed step by step: after PID reuse by a same-user process, `stop` SIGTERMs then SIGKILLs an unrelated process and its entire process group; after reuse by root/system (plausible post-reboot for low pids), kill returns EPERM on both targets, stop bails before mark_stopped, and start is refused at 537-544/595-603 because process_alive(EPERM)=true — the session is wedged Running until the occupying process exits or the user deletes the session dir. However, I downgrade severity from high to medium: when stale pids are NOT reused (the common case), ESRCH is tolerated, terminate_pid early-returns on !process_alive, and mark_stopped runs — stop degrades gracefully. All harmful variants require crash/reboot stale state PLUS PID-reuse coincidence, which is exactly the severity guide's medium bucket ("crashes leaving stale state"), and the wedge has a workaround (delete ~/.agent-bridge/sessions/<name>). The exit_status clobber alone is low-impact. This is not the roadmap-excluded "metadata recovery on daemon restart" item: the bug is stop's blind signaling of unverified pids, which fails in pure direct mode with no daemon involved.
verdict.fix: In stop_session_silent: return early (after ensuring mark_stopped) when metadata.status == Stopped. Record each process's start time at spawn (macOS: proc_pidinfo/PROC_PIDTBSDINFO pbi_start_tvsec via libproc; Linux: /proc/<pid>/stat field 22) in metadata, and before signaling verify the live process's start time matches; on mismatch or EPERM treat the target as already gone and still call mark_stopped instead of bailing. Clear child_pid/supervisor_pid in mark_stopped, and only send the group signal (-pid) when identity has been verified.

finding.title: read and read --raw fail permanently once a session log contains non-UTF-8 bytes
finding.detail: read_output_text uses fs::read_to_string on raw.log (exact PTY bytes, arbitrary) and clean.log (strip-ansi passes non-escape bytes through verbatim, so it is poisoned too). The first non-UTF-8 output — cat of a binary, git diff of a binary file, curl of a tarball, Latin-1 from a legacy tool, or a multibyte codepoint truncated mid-write/at kill — permanently poisons both append-only logs (no rotation): every subsequent `agent-bridge read NAME` and `read --raw`, direct or daemon-routed, fails with 'failed to read ... stream did not contain valid UTF-8' for the rest of the session's life. The manager agent loses all log access to a still-running session (only `screen` keeps working), and raw.log's documented exact-byte replay purpose is unmeetable through the CLI.
finding.fix: Read the file as bytes with fs::read and convert via String::from_utf8_lossy before splitting into lines.

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED for `read --raw` only; the clean.log half of the claim is refuted, so impact and severity are downgraded. Confirmed mechanism: capture_output (src/main.rs:789-792) appends exact PTY chunks to raw.log, which is truncated only at session start (initialize_session_files, line 647) and never rotated. Any non-UTF-8 program output (cat of a binary, curl of a tarball, Latin-1 tool) persists in raw.log for the session's life. read_output_text (src/main.rs:915-919) uses fs::read_to_string, which fails with InvalidData ("stream did not contain valid UTF-8") if any byte in the file is invalid; the tail parameter cannot avoid this because the whole file is read before tailing. Both the direct CLI path (Commands::Read, line 229) and the daemon path (DaemonRequest::Read, line 409) call this same function, so `agent-bridge read NAME --raw` is permanently broken for that session, defeating raw.log's exact-byte replay purpose through the CLI. REFUTED portion: the claim that clean.log "is poisoned too" because "strip-ansi passes non-escape bytes through verbatim" is false at the pinned versions. strip-ansi-escapes 0.2.1 (depends on vte 0.14.1 per Cargo.lock) produces output exclusively through Performer::print(c: char) — write!(writer, "{}", c), the UTF-8 encoding of a Rust char, valid by construction — and execute(), which writes only b'\n' (crate src/lib.rs lines 161-170); all other Perform methods are vte default no-ops (vte-0.14.1 src/lib.rs lines 763-812). vte 0.14.1 decodes UTF-8 itself, converting invalid or partial sequences to U+FFFD or skipping them (lib.rs lines 623-711; tests invalid_utf8, partial_invalid_utf8_split), so even a multibyte codepoint split across the 8 KiB chunk boundary (fresh parser per strip() call) cannot emit invalid bytes. clean.log is therefore always valid UTF-8: the default `read` keeps working (worst case a few U+FFFD), and `screen` works too (vt100 0.16.2 contents() is a String). The manager agent does NOT lose all log access — only the raw view. Severity: medium per the guide, which explicitly lists "binary output" under plausible-but-less-common conditions; normal everyday use (Claude Code, REPLs, dev servers emitting UTF-8 + ANSI escapes) never produces invalid UTF-8, and workarounds exist (default read, screen, reading the file directly).
verdict.fix: In read_output_text (src/main.rs:918-919), replace fs::read_to_string with let bytes = fs::read(&path)...; let contents = String::from_utf8_lossy(&bytes); then split into lines as before. This makes `read --raw` resilient to arbitrary PTY bytes (invalid sequences render as U+FFFD). Optionally apply the same pattern to read_screen_text for defense in depth; for true byte-exact replay, a future option is writing the tailed raw bytes to stdout without UTF-8 conversion.

finding.title: Session names '.', '..', and leading '-' pass validation: '..' scribbles session files into the bridge root, and '-name' breaks the direct-mode supervisor re-spawn
finding.detail: validate_session_name accepts any mix of [A-Za-z0-9._-] (src/main.rs:1199-1212), so '.', '..', and leading-dash names all pass. session_dir('..') = ~/.agent-bridge itself and session_dir('.') = the sessions root (src/main.rs:1303-1306). Verified: `agent-bridge start . --cmd "echo dot"` created raw.log, clean.log, screen.txt, metadata.json, and input.fifo directly in $AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME/sessions/; '..' targets the bridge root next to the live daemon socket, where initialize_session_files deletes/recreates input.fifo and truncates root-level files, and stop/status/read operate on bridge-root state — breaking the per-session sandbox and the documented sessions/{name} layout (such sessions are also invisible to `list`, which only enumerates subdirectories). It cannot reach arbitrary paths ('/' is blocked), but it clobbers root-level state. Separately, a name like '-foo' works in daemon mode but breaks direct mode: start_session_detached re-spawns `current_exe supervisor -foo ...` (src/main.rs:550-557) and clap in the supervisor rejects '-foo' as an unknown flag, so the supervisor exits instantly with stderr nulled, the client times out 'did not report running within 5 seconds', and metadata is stuck at Starting — the same name behaves differently depending on whether the daemon is up.
finding.fix: Reject '.', '..', and names starting with '-' (and ideally any leading dot); pass `--` before the name when spawning the supervisor; optionally canonicalize session_dir and assert the result stays strictly under sessions_root.

verdict.reasoning: Every mechanism in the claim checks out against the code. (1) validate_session_name (src/main.rs:1199-1212) only requires non-empty + bytes in [A-Za-z0-9._-], so '.', '..', and '-foo' all pass; session_dir (1303-1306) is a syntactic join onto sessions_root, and the kernel resolves '.'/'..' at syscall time, so session_dir("..") is the bridge root and session_dir(".") is the sessions root. create_dir_all on these succeeds (mkdir returns EEXIST, std falls back to path.is_dir()). initialize_session_files (637-663) then removes/recreates input.fifo and truncate-creates raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt/metadata.json directly in the bridge root (for '..', next to the 0600 daemon socket) or the sessions root (for '.'), breaking the sessions/{name} layout. list_sessions_text (996-1006) and shutdown_sessions_for_daemon (1039-1051) filter read_dir entries by is_dir() and readdir never yields dot entries, so such sessions are invisible to list and skipped by the daemon's shutdown stop-sweep, while status/read/stop by name operate on the bridge-root/sessions-root files (959-961, 1078-1080, 1176-1179). Additional verified interference: a '.' session leaves a plain file sessions/raw.log etc., which blocks any future legitimate session literally named raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt/metadata.json/input.fifo. Containment also holds as claimed: '/' is rejected, so reach is capped at the bridge root; socket perms 0600 (line 343) mean no cross-user boundary is involved. (2) '-foo' leg: clap is pinned at 4.6.1 where positionals default allow_hyphen_values=false, so the name is only expressible via 'start --cmd ... -- -foo'; the daemon path carries it as JSON and works (start_session_in_daemon never re-execs), but start_session_detached spawns `current_exe supervisor -foo --cwd ... --cmd ...` (550-557) with no '--' separator and stderr nulled (560); the supervisor's Cli::parse rejects '-foo' (unknown argument) and exits(2) before run_supervisor, metadata stays Starting, and wait_for_running_metadata (665-684) bails after 5s with "did not report running within 5 seconds", leaving sessions/-foo/metadata.json stuck at Starting — same name works in daemon mode, fails in direct mode. Severity downgraded from high to medium: normal everyday use (ordinary names) is unaffected, there is no cross-user exposure or escape beyond the bridge home, and triggering requires unusual deliberately supplied names ('.', '..', or a '--'-escaped leading-dash name) — this matches the guide's medium tier (failure under plausible-but-less-common conditions, stale state), not high (wrong behavior in normal everyday use).
verdict.fix: In validate_session_name (src/main.rs:1199), additionally reject name == "." , name == "..", and names starting with '-' (ideally any leading '.'). For defense in depth in start_session_detached (src/main.rs:550-557), move the name to the end of the supervisor argv behind a '--' separator — i.e. `supervisor --cwd <cwd> --cmd <cmd> -- <name>` — note that inserting '--' immediately before the name in its current first position would break parsing of the following --cwd/--cmd options, so it must go last. Optionally, after building session_dir, assert dir.parent() == Some(sessions_root) (or compare canonicalized paths) so any future validation gap cannot escape the per-session layout.

finding.title: screen.txt is rewritten with non-atomic fs::write on every PTY chunk, so concurrent screen reads see empty or truncated screens
finding.detail: capture_output calls fs::write(screen.txt) after every <=8KB read chunk (line 801) — open(O_TRUNC) then write, potentially dozens of times per second during active output — while read_screen_text (line 941) in another process does an uncoordinated read_to_string. A read landing between truncate and write successfully returns an empty or partial screen. This is the highest-frequency non-atomic-write race in the codebase and hits the primary documented use case directly: a manager AI polling `screen` during Claude streaming to decide whether the session is idle or awaiting approval intermittently observes a blank/truncated snapshot and acts on it (concludes the session is hung or dead, re-sends a prompt). The full-screen serialize+write per chunk is also wasted work under heavy output.
finding.fix: Write the snapshot to screen.txt.tmp and rename() over screen.txt (atomic on the same filesystem), optionally throttled (at most every N ms) to cut write amplification.

verdict.reasoning: Writer: capture_output (src/main.rs:768-807) calls fs::write(screen.txt, snapshot) on every PTY chunk (lines 801-805). std::fs::write = File::create (open O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC) then write_all — two separate syscalls, so the file is visibly 0 bytes between the truncating open and the write of the ≤3.7KB snapshot (30x120 screen, lines 35-36). Reader: read_screen_text (lines 937-952) does one uncoordinated fs::read_to_string (line 941) with no lock or retry; it runs in a different process in direct mode (CLI `screen`, line 230, vs supervisor process) and in a racing daemon thread in daemon mode (DaemonRequest::Screen handler, line 410). Grep confirms no flock, no tmp+rename, no other synchronization anywhere; line 649 creates the file, 801 is the only writer, 941 the only reader. Refutation attempts fail: (1) single-write atomicity is irrelevant because the empty window is between two syscalls (open(O_TRUNC) and write), not within one; (2) read_to_string returns Ok("") on an empty file, and lines 943-951 propagate it as a successful empty screen — the daemon replies ok:true and the CLI exits 0, so there is no error signal; trim_screen_snapshot (1152-1166) can legitimately produce "" for a blank screen, making race-induced blanks indistinguishable from real ones; (3) the writer rewrites tens-to-hundreds of times per second while a TUI like Claude Code repaints, the reader's own open-to-read gap widens the collision window (open pre-truncate, read post-truncate gives EOF at 0), and the documented use case is a manager AI polling `screen` over long-lived sessions, so occasional hits are statistically expected. Severity stays medium per the guide: an intermittent race causing silent wrong output (blank/torn screen acted on by the polling agent) under plausible-but-not-every-poll conditions, not a deterministic failure of normal use. The per-chunk full-screen rewrite is also real write amplification, but secondary.
verdict.fix: In capture_output, write the snapshot to a temp file in the same session directory (e.g. screen.txt.tmp) and fs::rename it over screen.txt; rename(2) atomically replaces the destination on the same filesystem, so readers always see a complete old or new snapshot. Optionally coalesce rewrites (e.g. at most once per 50-100 ms, or only when the PTY read would block) to cut write amplification. The same tmp+rename pattern should ideally be applied to save_metadata (line 1188), which has the identical non-atomic pattern at lower frequency.

finding.title: FIFO input writes are not atomic: O_NONBLOCK write_all delivers a partial prefix then errors on large sends, and concurrent senders can interleave mid-message
finding.detail: write_session_bytes opens input.fifo O_WRONLY|O_NONBLOCK (line 880) and calls write_all (line 884); std's write_all does not retry WouldBlock. The FIFO holds ~16-64KB and forward_input drains it at the child's pace through the small PTY input queue, so a large send (multi-KB pasted diff/prompt — the tool's core Phase-2 use case) to a busy or non-reading child (REPL computing, dev server that never reads stdin, child suspended via `keys ctrl-z`) fills the buffer and write returns EAGAIN after partial progress: the CLI errors 'failed to write input' (with no byte count) AFTER an arbitrary prefix was already delivered, the child executes truncated input without the trailing \r, and the natural manager retry delivers the prefix twice. Pipe writes are atomic only up to PIPE_BUF (512 bytes on macOS), so two concurrent senders (two daemon threads, or daemon thread + --direct CLI) with larger payloads can also interleave bytes mid-message, splicing two prompts together.
finding.fix: Keep the O_NONBLOCK open only as the no-reader (ENXIO) probe, then clear O_NONBLOCK via fcntl (or loop on WouldBlock with a deadline) before writing; chunk to <=PIPE_BUF or serialize senders per session; report bytes written on failure.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code walk-through of /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) write_session_bytes opens input.fifo with .write(true).custom_flags(libc::O_NONBLOCK) (lines 878-881); custom_flags ORs O_NONBLOCK into open(2) flags, so it persists as a file-status flag governing every write on the fd, not just the open. (2) Line 884 calls std write_all, which retries only ErrorKind::Interrupted; WouldBlock (EAGAIN) aborts immediately, and bytes written by earlier loop iterations remain in the FIFO. (3) POSIX/macOS semantics: nonblocking writes > PIPE_BUF (512 bytes on macOS) transfer what fits and return partial counts; a completely full buffer yields EAGAIN; atomicity holds only <= 512 bytes. (4) The sole drainer, forward_input (lines 809-830), reads 8KB chunks and does a blocking write_all to the PTY master, which stalls on the ~1KB macOS tty input queue when the child is not reading (busy REPL, dev server ignoring stdin, or child suspended via the explicitly supported `keys ctrl-z`, line 896). The FIFO (finite kernel buffer, single-digit-to-tens of KB on macOS) then fills. Even with a reading child, the sender's tight syscall loop outruns the millisecond-scale PTY drain, and a single full-buffer instant aborts the whole send, so any payload larger than FIFO capacity delivers an arbitrary prefix then errors `failed to write input` (no byte count, line 884 context). The lost tail includes the trailing \r appended at line 842; a manager retry duplicates the prefix. No retry, lock, chunking, or rollback exists anywhere in the file. Concurrency sub-claim also holds: the daemon handles each request on its own thread (line 350) with no shared state, and --direct CLI processes (line 203) bypass the daemon, so multiple fds can write the same FIFO concurrently; >512-byte writes are not atomic and can interleave mid-message. Attempted refutations failed: O_NONBLOCK is not open-only; the O_RDWR holder prevents ENXIO/EOF but not buffer exhaustion; sends <=512 bytes fail cleanly (atomic) but the claim concerns multi-KB sends. Only minor inaccuracy: macOS FIFO capacity is likely below the claimed 16-64KB, which makes failure easier, not harder. Severity stays medium per the guide: short interactive sends to a reading child (the everyday path) work; failure requires large inputs or a non-reading child — exactly the guide's plausible-but-less-common bucket.
verdict.fix: In write_session_bytes, keep O_NONBLOCK only for the open (no-reader probe), then clear it with fcntl(fd, F_SETFL, flags & ~O_NONBLOCK) before writing — or loop on ErrorKind::WouldBlock with a short sleep and an overall deadline instead of plain write_all. To preserve atomicity against concurrent senders, either chunk writes to <= PIPE_BUF (512 bytes) or serialize sends per session (per-session mutex in the daemon plus an flock on the session dir for --direct). On any failure, report how many bytes were already delivered.

finding.title: --direct start silently clobbers a live daemon-owned session of the same name, orphaning its child (asymmetric liveness check)
finding.detail: start_session_detached's already-running guard (src/main.rs:537-545) only bails if supervisor_pid is alive, but daemon-owned sessions always have supervisor_pid=None — the daemon path correctly checks child_pid instead (src/main.rs:595-602). So `agent-bridge --direct start name` against a live daemon session — a documented debugging workflow — passes deterministically, no race timing required. Verified: with daemon-owned 'py' (child 38402) Running, `--direct start py` succeeded; metadata repointed to new pids 38408/38409 and python 38402 stayed alive but untracked — `stop` can never reach it again. initialize_session_files deletes the live FIFO and truncates raw/clean/screen logs while the daemon's capture thread holds open append fds, so two PTYs interleave into one raw.log/clean.log and fight over screen.txt; when the daemon's owner thread later sees its child exit it calls mark_stopped, clobbering the new supervisor session's Running metadata so send/status report 'not running' for a session that is alive. The same hole fires without --direct after a daemon crash (socket gone so commands go direct, but the HUP-surviving child is still alive).
finding.fix: Use the same liveness predicate in both paths: consider the session running if status==Running and EITHER supervisor_pid OR child_pid is alive.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly against /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) The direct-path guard (lines 537-545) bails only if status==Running AND supervisor_pid is alive; the daemon-path guard (lines 595-603) tests child_pid instead. (2) Daemon-owned sessions always have supervisor_pid=None: initialize_session_files writes None (line 656) and the daemon's in-thread supervise_pty (lines 610-619, 738-743) sets only status/child_pid; supervisor_pid is set solely by run_supervisor (line 690) on the direct path. So for a live daemon session the direct guard evaluates `true && None.is_some_and(..)` = false and passes deterministically — no race timing involved. (3) `--direct` skips daemon routing entirely (main, lines 203-222), and a crashed daemon (socket NotFound/ConnectionRefused, lines 282-283) routes even non---direct commands to the same path. (4) initialize_session_files (lines 637-663) then unlinks the live FIFO (orphaning the daemon's forward_input thread and the old child's input path), O_TRUNCs raw/clean/screen on the same inodes the daemon's capture thread holds O_APPEND fds to (lines 769-778) — so two PTYs interleave into one raw.log/clean.log and both fs::write screen.txt (line 801) — and rewrites metadata so the old child's pid is tracked nowhere; stop_session_silent (lines 1078-1102) can only signal the new pids, and the old child sits in its own setsid session so the -pid group kill cannot reach it. (5) When the old child eventually exits, the daemon thread's mark_stopped (lines 616, 1168-1174) loads the CURRENT metadata — now the new live session — and flips it to Stopped, after which send bails "not running" (lines 873-875) for an alive session. No countervailing mechanism exists (no lock file, no cross-check, mkfifo succeeds on the fresh path, wait_for_running_metadata observes the new supervisor normally). Severity stays medium: everyday single-mode use is unaffected (each guard is self-consistent within its own mode); the failure requires the cross-mode collision via the documented --direct bypass or a daemon crash leaving a SIGHUP-surviving child — plausible but less common conditions per the guide.
verdict.fix: Use one shared liveness predicate in both start paths, e.g. add `fn session_is_active(m: &SessionMetadata) -> bool { m.status == SessionStatus::Running && (m.supervisor_pid.is_some_and(|p| process_alive(p as i32)) || m.child_pid.is_some_and(|p| process_alive(p as i32))) }` and call it from both start_session_detached (src/main.rs:537-545) and start_session_in_daemon (src/main.rs:595-603), bailing "session already running" if it returns true.

finding.title: start-vs-start TOCTOU: the check-init-spawn sequence has no lock and a session in Starting status never blocks a second start
finding.detail: Both start paths do load_metadata -> check -> initialize_session_files -> spawn with no lock (direct: src/main.rs:537-575; daemon: 595-619 — thread-per-connection with no shared in-memory registry), and the check only bails when status==Running: a session in Starting (the entire spawn window, plus forever if a previous supervisor crashed pre-Running) is not protected at all. Two concurrent `start foo` (a manager-agent retry after the 5s wait timeout or a torn-metadata parse failure, parallel tool calls, or two agents) both pass the check: the second deletes the first's FIFO and truncates the logs mid-setup (lines 641-649, with a possible mkfifo EEXIST race between the two removes), and two supervisors/owner-threads each spawn a child. Result: two children of the same name; metadata records only the last writer's pids; both capture threads interleave bytes into the same raw.log/clean.log and alternately rewrite screen.txt with different vt100 states; sends reach only the instance holding the new FIFO; and `stop` kills only the recorded pids — the other child and its supervisor leak untracked and keep scribbling the logs, while both waiting clients report 'started' from whichever metadata won.
finding.fix: Take an exclusive per-session advisory lock (flock/O_EXCL lock file in the session dir, or a daemon-side named-mutex registry) around check+initialize+spawn, and treat Starting-with-live-owner as already running.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by full read of /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) Neither start path holds any lock: grep finds no flock/Mutex/O_EXCL/create_new anywhere; the daemon (run_daemon, lines 347-358) is thread-per-connection with no shared registry, and each Start blocks up to 5s in wait_for_running_metadata, so two Start requests provably overlap. (2) The already-running check (direct 537-545, daemon 595-603) bails only on status==Running AND a live recorded pid; initialize_session_files (651-662) writes status=Starting with both pids None, so the entire spawn window passes the check — and on a first-ever start load_metadata errors and `if let Ok` skips the check entirely, so two simultaneous first starts both proceed unconditionally. (3) The destructive interleaving is real: the second starter's initialize_session_files (641-649) deletes the first's FIFO and re-creates it (or hits the claimed mkfifo EEXIST race if both pass exists() first), and File::create truncates raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt while the first owner's append-mode FDs (capture_output 768-778) keep writing the same inode. (4) Both owners spawn a child (supervise_pty 732-735); metadata is plain fs::write last-writer-wins (1183-1188), so one (supervisor_pid, child_pid) pair is lost (read-modify-write interleavings can even mix pids across the two instances via run_supervisor 689-692 vs supervise_pty 738-743). Both capture threads interleave the logs and alternately rewrite screen.txt from two vt100 parsers (800-805); sends open the FIFO by path (877-882) and reach whichever owner(s) opened the current FIFO (809-814); both waiting clients poll the same metadata.json and both print "started" (665-684). (5) stop_session_silent (1078-1102) signals only the recorded pids; the unrecorded supervisor (own setsid session, 564-571) and child (own session via portable-pty) leak untracked and keep scribbling logs; even daemon Shutdown (1032-1069) misses them since it goes by metadata. Refutation attempts failed: there is no serialization point, the loser does not reliably fail benignly (mkfifo EEXIST is only one narrow interleave), and the triggers are realistic for this tool's stated user (manager-AI parallel tool calls; a retry after the 5s timeout at line 683 races the still-detached supervisor). Severity stays medium per the guide: requires a concurrent-start race (plausible-but-less-common), but yields process leaks, corrupted/interleaved logs, misrouted input, and a stop that does not fully stop.
verdict.fix: In both start_session_detached and start_session_in_daemon, take an exclusive advisory lock before the metadata check and hold it through wait_for_running_metadata: open session_dir/start.lock and flock(LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB) (via nix::fcntl::Flock or libc::flock on the File fd); if the lock is held, bail "session '<name>' is already starting". Because the lock is held until the session reaches Running (or the start fails), a concurrent start either blocks/bails on the lock or arrives after Running and is caught by the existing status check. Keep allowing a stale Starting record (crashed supervisor) to be restarted once the lock is acquired. A daemon-only Mutex<HashSet<name>> is insufficient because direct mode (--direct or no daemon) bypasses the daemon process; the per-session lock file covers both paths and mixed mode.

finding.title: stop during session startup is silently lost: no status gate, pids not yet recorded, and the owner later unconditionally writes Running
finding.detail: stop_session_silent (line 1078) kills only the pids currently in metadata and then marks Stopped — it never gates on status or prevents a future transition. If stop lands while the session is Starting (daemon mode: child_pid and supervisor_pid both None until line 743; direct mode: before run_supervisor writes its pid at 692), it kills nothing, reports 'stopped session' success, and writes Stopped; the owner thread/supervisor then proceeds to spawn the child and unconditionally sets status=Running (supervise_pty lines 740-743), resurrecting the session the manager believes is dead. Worse, the concurrently-waiting start client can load the transient Stopped and report 'session stopped while starting: stopped by user' — leaving a session the manager believes both failed and stopped, but which is actually running. In direct mode there is also a leak variant: stop kills the supervisor (line 1090) in the window after spawn_command returns but before child_pid is saved (lines 732-743); the child was setsid'd into its own process group, so kill(-supervisor) never reaches it and its pid was never recorded — an orphaned interactive child the tool can no longer stop.
finding.fix: Record pids before/atomically with spawn and make stop set a 'stop requested' marker (or Stopped status) that the owner re-checks before promoting the session to Running.

verdict.reasoning: Verified step-by-step against /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) stop_session_silent (1078-1102) has no status gate: it loads metadata once, kills child_pid/supervisor_pid only if Some, then unconditionally writes Stopped/"stopped by user" and returns Ok, so the caller reports "stopped session" success (414/1073). (2) During startup both pids are None: initialize_session_files (651-662) writes Starting/None/None; in daemon mode child_pid is first recorded only at supervise_pty 738-743 after spawn_command (732-735); the daemon is thread-per-connection with no locking (350, 394-434), so a concurrent Stop lands freely in this window, kills nothing, writes Stopped, and reports success. (3) Resurrection is real: supervise_pty loads metadata at 738 and unconditionally sets status=Running at 740 and saves at 743 with no re-check, overwriting the Stopped written by stop — the session the manager believes dead is running. (4) wait_for_running_metadata (665-684) polls at 100ms and bails "stopped while starting: stopped by user" if it observes the transient Stopped, so the start client can simultaneously report failure while the session ends up Running. (5) Direct mode has the same resurrection window before run_supervisor saves supervisor_pid at 692. The 692-743 leak variant is mechanically correct but overstated: verified in vendored portable-pty 0.8.1 (src/unix.rs:220) that the PTY child setsid()s into its own process group, so kill(-supervisor_pid) (signal_pid_and_group, 1137) never reaches it and its pid was never recorded; however controlling_tty defaults to true (cmdbuilder.rs:216) and SIGHUP is reset to SIG_DFL pre-exec, so when the SIGTERM'd supervisor exits the closing PTY master delivers SIGHUP to the child's foreground pgrp and typically kills it — a permanent untracked orphan requires a SIGHUP-ignoring program. Severity stays medium per the guide: it is a race confined to the ~5-50ms spawn window, unreachable by a sequential manager that waits for start to return, and in most interleavings recoverable (child_pid is recorded at 743 and stop has no status gate, so a second stop works; list/status expose the inconsistency). But concurrent stop-during-start is plausible for an AI manager (cancel-during-start, parallel tool calls), and the result — stop reports success while the session keeps running — is real wrong behavior.
verdict.fix: Two small changes. (a) In supervise_pty, gate the promotion to Running: after spawn_command, when re-loading metadata (line 738), if status == Stopped (stop requested during startup), kill the just-spawned child (terminate_pid on child.process_id()) and return "stopped during startup" instead of writing Running. Apply the same status re-check in run_supervisor before/when saving supervisor_pid at 692. (b) In stop_session_silent, when the loaded status is Starting and both pids are None, do not silently succeed: briefly poll (e.g. up to ~2s) for pids to appear and kill them, or bail with "session is still starting; retry" — and still write Stopped so the owner's re-check in (a) aborts the spawn.

finding.title: Daemon shutdown calls std::process::exit(0) while request threads and session owners are mid-flight, and skips Starting sessions entirely
finding.detail: shutdown_sessions_for_daemon stops only sessions whose metadata says Running at scan time (src/main.rs:1032-1069); handle_daemon_stream then calls std::process::exit(0) (line 388), killing all daemon threads at arbitrary points. A Start racing the shutdown (child spawned after the scan, or status still Starting) is skipped by the scan and its owner thread is destroyed by exit(0): a live PTY child is orphaned with no FIFO reader, metadata is permanently stuck at Starting/Running, subsequent send fails ENXIO and status lies. Any concurrent Stop/Send thread killed inside save_metadata's truncate-write window leaves corrupt metadata.json on disk, and other connected clients get their sockets dropped mid-response ('daemon closed connection without a response') with no way to know whether their command applied. Inverse ordering bug: if writing the shutdown response fails (client gone), the `?` at line 381 returns before exit(0), leaving a daemon that already killed every session but keeps running.
finding.fix: Serialize shutdown with in-flight requests (refuse new connections, drain active handlers, stop Starting sessions too — waiting briefly or killing them) before exiting, instead of process::exit(0) mid-write.

verdict.reasoning:
Every mechanical element of the claim checks out in /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs.

(1) Running-only scan: shutdown_sessions_for_daemon (1032-1069) collects only sessions with `metadata.status == SessionStatus::Running` (line 1046). Sessions in Starting are skipped, and sessions whose dir appears after the read_dir scan are invisible. The Starting window is real, not theoretical: start_session_in_daemon writes Starting metadata (initialize_session_files, line 655/662), then the owner thread spawns the PTY child (732-735) BEFORE flipping metadata to Running (740-743) — so a live child can exist while status is Starting.

(2) Hard exit with no coordination: handle_daemon_stream calls std::process::exit(0) at line 388. The daemon has no Mutex/flag/listener-close anywhere (grep confirms only a `&'static [u8]` type hit); each connection is a bare thread::spawn (350). std::process::exit terminates all threads at arbitrary points without running destructors. The race window is wide: terminate_pid (1104-1131) waits up to ~4s per session (2s post-SIGTERM + 2s post-SIGKILL polling), so a concurrent Start can fully complete — client told success — during the shutdown's stop loop, then be orphaned by exit(0).

(3) Consequences verified: the orphaned session's owner/forward_input/capture_output threads die; nothing ever calls mark_stopped (616), so metadata is permanently Starting/Running with supervisor_pid None. status_text (959-983) then prints `status: Running` from stale metadata (it does also print child_alive=false as a hint). send via write_session_bytes passes the Running check (873) and opens the FIFO O_WRONLY|O_NONBLOCK (878-882); with the daemon's forward_input reader (O_RDWR holder, 810-814) dead, POSIX open returns ENXIO → "failed to open input FIFO". One nuance: "live PTY child is orphaned" is slightly overstated — portable-pty 0.8 children are session leaders with the slave as controlling tty, so closing the master at process exit delivers SIGHUP and most children die; only SIGHUP-ignoring children persist. The stuck-metadata/lying-status/ENXIO damage holds either way. Recovery is manual but possible (stop, or re-start same name — line 595-603 allows it since the child is dead).

(4) Metadata corruption window: save_metadata (1183-1189) uses fs::write = open(O_TRUNC)+write_all, non-atomic. This race is structurally in flight on every shutdown of daemon-owned sessions: stop_session_silent's terminate_pid returns the instant the child dies, while the session's owner thread concurrently wakes from child.wait() (760) and calls mark_stopped (616); exit(0) can land between truncate and write, leaving an empty metadata.json. Window is microseconds (low probability per shutdown) and corruption is recoverable via re-start (595 `if let Ok` tolerates parse failure), so this sub-point is real but minor.

(5) Dropped clients: exit(0) closes all sockets; in-flight clients hit EOF in try_send_daemon_request → "daemon closed connection without a response" (301-302), with genuine ambiguity about whether e.g. a Send already reached the FIFO.

(6) Inverse ordering: handle_daemon_request (380) executes all the session kills before the response write; Rust std ignores SIGPIPE, so if the shutdown client vanished, serde_json::to_writer at 381 returns Err(EPIPE) and `?` propagates, skipping exit(0) at 388 — daemon stays alive having killed every session. Uncommon (client must die during the multi-second stop loop) and recoverable by re-issuing shutdown, but the ordering is verifiably inverted.

Not excluded by the NOT-findings list: "metadata recovery on daemon restart" covers recovering after a crash; this finding is about the implemented orderly-shutdown path itself skipping live sessions and hard-exiting mid-flight — the Shutdown handler explicitly tries to stop sessions first, showing clean shutdown is implemented-but-buggy, not absent roadmap work.

Severity: medium fits the guide exactly — failures require concurrency at the shutdown instant or a vanished client (races leaving stale state/orphans); normal quiescent shutdown works. Not high (everyday use unaffected), not low (lying status, dead-but-Running sessions, possible corrupt metadata).
verdict.fix: Replace the bare std::process::exit(0) with an orderly shutdown sequence in the daemon: (a) on Shutdown, set a shared shutdown flag and stop accepting new connections (close/drop the listener or check the flag in the accept loop); (b) track in-flight request threads (e.g., Arc<(Mutex<usize>, Condvar)> incremented per connection) and wait for them to drain before stopping sessions; (c) in shutdown_sessions_for_daemon, include SessionStatus::Starting sessions — re-scan until no Running/Starting sessions remain, briefly waiting for Starting ones to publish child_pid and then terminating them; (d) write the response, then exit by returning from the accept loop normally (and if the response write fails, log and proceed with exit anyway rather than aborting via `?`, fixing the inversion at src/main.rs:381-388). Independently, make save_metadata atomic (write to metadata.json.tmp then fs::rename) so any abrupt termination cannot leave a truncated metadata.json.

finding.title: AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME is trusted verbatim and the docs point it at world-writable /tmp, enabling symlink/pre-creation attacks
finding.detail: bridge_root() returns AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME unchecked, and run_daemon/initialize_session_files then create_dir_all it and File::create / fs::write inside it, all following symlinks. The README and design doc explicitly instruct `AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME=/private/tmp/agent-bridge-test`. In sticky world-writable /tmp, another local user can pre-create that path (or interior files like sessions/<name>/raw.log) as a symlink before the owner runs; create_dir_all/File::create/fs::write then follow the link, letting the attacker (a) own the directory the victim writes secret logs into and read them, or (b) redirect a log/screen/metadata write through a symlink to clobber a victim-writable file. Even with no symlink, an attacker-pre-created dir is attacker-owned yet used by the victim daemon.
finding.anchor: bridge_root src/main.rs:1222 (consumed by run_daemon src/main.rs:330 and initialize_session_files src/main.rs:637)
finding.fix: Reject a root whose parent is world-writable or whose existing path is not owned by the current uid; create the tree with 0700 and O_NOFOLLOW semantics, and change the docs to use a per-user location instead of /tmp.

verdict.reasoning:
Every load-bearing assertion in the claim checks out against the real code and macOS platform semantics; I tried to refute it on five fronts and could not.

CODE FACTS (all verified in /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs):
- bridge_root() lines 1222-1228 returns `PathBuf::from(AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME)` verbatim — no lstat, no symlink check, no ownership check, no world-writable-parent check.
- run_daemon line 330 does `fs::create_dir_all(&root)` with no explicit mode. Session start does the same for `sessions/<name>` at lines 534 and 592. std's create_dir_all returns Ok when the path already exists as a directory OR a symlink to a directory (it falls back to `path.is_dir()`, which follows symlinks) and never inspects ownership or removes the existing entry.
- initialize_session_files lines 647-649 create raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt via `File::create` with no prior existence check and no O_NOFOLLOW/O_EXCL. File::create = create+truncate+write, which follows symlinks. save_metadata line 1188 and the per-chunk screen write at lines 801-805 use `fs::write`, same follow-symlink + truncate semantics.
- The ONLY permission hardening is the socket (0600, line 343) and the fifo (0600 plus pre-removal of any existing entry, lines 641-645). Logs and metadata.json get default perms.

PLATFORM FACTS (verified on this box): /private/tmp is drwxrwxrwt (mode 1777 — sticky + world-writable); default umask is 022, so create_dir_all yields 0755 dirs and File::create/fs::write yield 0644 (world-readable) files. The sticky bit means the victim's process cannot remove or replace an entry another uid pre-created (and the code never tries to).

DOCS FACT: README.md:20 instructs `AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME=/private/tmp/agent-bridge-test ./target/debug/agent-bridge daemon` — the daemon that creates the root and owns sessions — and README.md:41 / docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md:71 repeat the same /private/tmp path for other commands.

CONCRETE FAILURE SCENARIOS:
(A) Confidentiality, no race needed: a user following the documented test workflow (export AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME=/private/tmp/agent-bridge-test; daemon + start + send) gets root 0755 and raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt/metadata.json all 0644 inside world-readable /private/tmp. Any other local uid can `cat /private/tmp/agent-bridge-test/sessions/*/raw.log` and read the victim's full terminal session output (Claude Code / REPL / shell content, file contents, command output, any secrets echoed) and the command line (metadata.json contains the parsed command). Secrets are exposed beyond the owning user.
(B) Durable pre-creation hijack + symlink clobber: a second local uid pre-creates /private/tmp/agent-bridge-test as a directory it owns. Because /tmp is sticky, the victim cannot displace it and create_dir_all (line 330) silently reuses it; nothing checks ownership. The attacker now controls the tree, can create sessions/<name>/ (guessing/observing names like the doc's `py`), and plant raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt/metadata.json as symlinks to victim-writable files. The victim's File::create (647-649) and fs::write (801, 1188) follow those symlinks and truncate/overwrite the targets (the screen snapshot content is even partly attacker-influenceable via injected input). The fifo is the one exception (pre-removed at 641-643 then mkfifo'd).

REFUTATIONS CONSIDERED AND REJECTED: (1) "User sets their own env var, so verbatim trust is fine" — true in isolation, but the finding is the combination of world-readable secret files plus official docs pointing storage at sticky /tmp; the code genuinely contributes (no perm tightening, no O_NOFOLLOW, no ownership guard). (2) "create_dir_all wouldn't reuse a symlink/foreign dir" — it does; it only checks is_dir() and never ownership. (3) "File::create uses O_EXCL/O_NOFOLLOW" — it does not. (4) "Single-user macOS laptop has no attacker" — correct that this needs a multi-user/shared/CI host, which is exactly why medium (plausible-but-less-common) is the right rating, not high/critical; the safe default (~/.agent-bridge) means the exposure only manifests under the documented /tmp override. (5) "Pre-creation is a tight race" — it is not; the sticky bit makes the attacker's entry durable, so they plant it once before the victim ever runs.

Net: the mechanism is real and matches the code line-for-line. Severity stays medium: the default config is safe, so triggering requires the non-default (test-oriented but documented) /tmp override AND a multi-user host; the pure confidentiality leg (world-readable logs in /tmp) brushes the "secrets exposed beyond owning user" high bar but is gated by that same conditionality.
verdict.fix: Three-part fix. (1) Harden bridge_root() in src/main.rs:1222: after resolving an explicit AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME, `lstat` the path and bail if it is a symlink or exists but is not owned by the current uid (use std::os::unix MetadataExt::uid via symlink_metadata), and refuse a root whose parent is world-writable unless it is sticky-and-owned; reject relative paths. (2) Create the tree privately and without following symlinks: create the root and sessions/<name> dirs with mode 0700 (DirBuilder::mode(0o700)), and create raw.log/clean.log/screen.txt/metadata.json with OpenOptions().create_new(true).custom_flags(libc::O_NOFOLLOW).mode(0o600) (or chmod the parent 0700 and reject any pre-existing non-owned entry) so a planted symlink or foreign file is rejected rather than followed/truncated. (3) Change README.md:20/41 and docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md:71 to a per-user location — on macOS `$TMPDIR` (verified here as /var/folders/.../T mode 0700, owned by the user) or `$HOME/.agent-bridge-test`, never the shared /private/tmp.

finding.title: Daemon has no read timeout or request-size limit and spawns an unbounded thread per connection (local DoS)
finding.detail: run_daemon accepts each connection and thread::spawn's a handler with no cap; handle_daemon_stream does a single read_line with no timeout and no length bound. A local process that can reach the socket can (a) open many connections and never send a newline — each handler blocks forever in read_line, permanently pinning one thread per connection until the daemon exhausts threads/FDs/memory, or (b) send one enormous line with no \n, making read_line buffer it unbounded into memory (OOM). Either takes down the daemon and thus the control plane for all of the user's sessions. The socket's 0600 perms limit the attacker to the same user today, but the protocol is otherwise unauthenticated, so any same-user process (e.g. a compromised dependency) suffices.
finding.fix: Set read/write timeouts on the accepted stream, bound the request with a length-limited reader (read_line over a take()-capped reader), and cap concurrent handler threads.

verdict.reasoning:
The mechanics are real. run_daemon (src/main.rs:347-358) spawns one thread per accepted connection with no cap. handle_daemon_stream (src/main.rs:363-392) never sets read/write timeouts (no set_read_timeout/set_write_timeout anywhere in the file; std UnixStream defaults to blocking with no timeout), so read_line at line 371 blocks forever for a client that connects and withholds the newline, pinning a thread and FDs per connection (macOS default soft RLIMIT_NOFILE of 256 means a few hundred such connections make accept() fail; the Err arm at line 356 just prints and keeps looping). BufRead::read_line also appends unboundedly, so a giant newline-free stream grows daemon memory without limit. The symmetric write side is also unbounded (serde_json::to_writer at line 381 can block forever on a client that never reads a large Read/Screen response). None of this is prevented anywhere.

Severity is overstated though. (1) The socket is chmod 0600 (line 343), so only same-UID processes can connect — no cross-user boundary is involved. (2) A same-user attacker already has a strictly easier, designed-in kill switch: the unauthenticated DaemonRequest::Shutdown makes the daemon std::process::exit(0) (lines 379, 387-388), and plain kill(2) or deleting ~/.agent-bridge work too — so the missing limits grant an attacker (including the claim's "compromised dependency") no capability they lack. (3) No accidental trigger exists: the legitimate client (try_send_daemon_request, lines 290-294) writes the complete serde_json frame plus "\n" and flushes immediately after connect (serde_json escapes embedded newlines so the frame is one line, bounded by argv/ARG_MAX ~1 MB), and any client that crashes, disconnects, or merely probes the socket (e.g. the stale-socket check at line 334) closes its FD so read_line returns EOF and the handler exits via the empty-line bail (lines 373-374). Normal everyday use can never block a handler or grow the buffer. Per the severity guide this is "resource hygiene, hardening" with an easy workaround (restart the daemon): low, downgraded from the claimed medium.
verdict.fix: In handle_daemon_stream, set stream.set_read_timeout(Some(Duration::from_secs(10))) and set_write_timeout(...) on the accepted UnixStream before reading, and bound the request line by wrapping the cloned stream in Read::take(1 << 20) before BufReader::read_line, bailing if the cap is reached without a trailing newline. Optionally cap concurrent handler threads (simple counting semaphore), though with timeouts in place blocked handlers self-expire.

finding.title: Stateless per-chunk ANSI stripping corrupts clean.log whenever an escape sequence splits across an 8KB read boundary
finding.detail: capture_output calls strip_ansi_escapes::strip(chunk) per read; strip() constructs a fresh vte parser per call (verified in strip-ansi-escapes 0.2.1 source), so parser state is discarded between chunks. An escape sequence straddling the 8192-byte buffer boundary is half-dropped, half-emitted as literal text: a chunk ending '\x1b[38;5;2' is swallowed mid-parse and the next chunk's '40m' is written to clean.log as visible text. Claude Code and other TUI targets emit escape-dense multi-KB redraw bursts constantly, so chunk boundaries land inside sequences routinely, sprinkling fragments like '40m' and ';5;240m' through clean.log — the default source for `read` — misleading the manager agent that parses it and contradicting the doc's 'readable append-only text' claim.
finding.fix: Keep one stateful stripping parser alive across chunks (persistent vte::Parser/strip Writer, buffer a trailing partial escape between reads, or derive clean text from the persistent vt100 parser) instead of calling stateless strip() per chunk.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code and both pinned crates' sources (no build/run needed).

Code under test (/Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs:780-806): capture_output reads the PTY into a fixed 8192-byte buffer and calls strip_ansi_escapes::strip(chunk) per chunk (line 794), appending the result to clean.log. The vt100 parser used for screen.txt is persistent across chunks (line 779); only the clean.log path is stateless.

strip-ansi-escapes 0.2.1 (pinned in Cargo.lock; source at ~/.cargo/registry/src/.../strip-ansi-escapes-0.2.1/src/lib.rs): strip() constructs a fresh Writer over a Vec, which constructs a fresh vte::Parser::new() (lib.rs lines 62-73, 106-114), and drops it on return. Its Performer emits output only via print() (printable chars) and execute(b'\n'); csi_dispatch/esc_dispatch/osc_dispatch are no-ops. So any bytes consumed into escape-parsing states produce nothing, and the in-flight state is discarded between chunks.

vte 0.14.1 (the version strip-ansi-escapes 0.2.1 resolves to per Cargo.lock; source verified): a stateful Paul Williams state machine whose `state: State` and `partial_utf8` fields persist across advance() calls within one Parser instance — exactly the state strip() throws away.

Step-by-step failure walk-through with chunk1 ending "...\x1b[38;5;2" and chunk2 starting "40m...":
1. strip(chunk1): preceding text printed; 0x1B switches to State::Escape (advance_ground); '[' to CsiEntry; "38;5;2" to CsiParam, collected into params with no performer callback (csi_dispatch fires only on a final byte 0x40-0x7E). advance() returns with state=CsiParam; the Writer/Parser is dropped; the partial sequence is silently swallowed (harmless by itself).
2. strip(chunk2): a brand-new Parser starts in Ground. advance_ground's memchr finds no ESC in "40m"; from_utf8 succeeds; ground_dispatch (vte lib.rs:722-728) calls print('4'), print('0'), print('m') — the fragment "40m" is appended to clean.log as literal visible text.

Same root cause also corrupts split UTF-8: advance_ground stashes a trailing partial codepoint in self.partial_utf8 for the NEXT advance call, but strip() discards it; the following chunk's orphan continuation bytes then hit the invalid-UTF-8 path and print('\u{FFFD}') replacement characters.

Trigger plausibility: read boundaries are byte-offset determined (the 8192 cap whenever the kernel tty queue holds more, or wherever the writer/reader race stands), never aligned to escape sequences. The project's headline target (Claude Code, full-screen TUI) emits escape-dense multi-KB redraw bursts, so boundaries routinely land mid-sequence. Impact surface: read_output_text (main.rs:917) serves clean.log by default (raw is opt-in) in both direct and daemon modes (main.rs:229, 261-264, 409), so the manager agent's default read view accumulates fragments like "40m" / ";5;240m" and stray replacement chars.

No mitigating mechanism exists: no carry-over buffering, no realignment, and screen.txt's correct persistent parser is a separate artifact.

Severity stays medium: real corruption with an everyday trigger, but it only sprinkles fragments into one derived artifact; raw.log is intact, screen.txt (the appropriate view for TUI targets, and rendered by the correctly-persistent vt100 parser) is unaffected, and `read --raw` is an immediate workaround. Stripped TUI redraw streams have limited fidelity even with perfect stripping (positioning is lost), so the marginal harm is misleading fragments rather than loss of otherwise-clean data.
verdict.fix: In capture_output (src/main.rs), replace the per-chunk strip() call with one persistent stripping writer created before the read loop, so the vte parser state (including partial escape sequences and partial UTF-8) carries across chunk boundaries: `let mut clean_writer = strip_ansi_escapes::Writer::new(clean_log);` then per chunk `clean_writer.write_all(chunk)?; clean_writer.flush()?;` (flush is needed each iteration because Writer wraps the sink in a LineWriter). Alternatively, derive clean.log content from the already-persistent vt100 parser, but the persistent Writer is the minimal change.

finding.title: read loads the entire unbounded log into memory to tail a few lines
finding.detail: read_output_text reads the whole raw.log/clean.log into a String, then materializes a Vec<&str> of every line, just to print the last `tail` (default 300) lines. These logs grow without bound for the life of a session, and Claude-style TUIs rewrite the screen continuously, so raw.log reaches hundreds of MB within hours and GBs over days. Every `read` then allocates the entire file (twice: string plus line vector) — and in daemon mode this happens inside the durable daemon process on every poll of the manager's loop, with concurrent reads multiplying the spike: multi-second latencies, memory bloat, and eventual allocation failure/OOM kill of the daemon, which takes down every running session's ownership thread.
finding.fix: Seek backward from EOF reading bounded blocks until `tail` newlines are found (or cap the bytes read), instead of read_to_string on the whole file.

verdict.reasoning: Anchor verified: read_output_text (src/main.rs:915-930) calls fs::read_to_string on the whole raw.log/clean.log (lines 918-919), then materializes Vec<&str> of every line (line 921, ~16 bytes/line) to emit only the last `tail` lines (default 300, line 83). Growth side verified: capture_output (src/main.rs:768-807) appends every PTY chunk to raw.log and the ANSI-stripped chunk to clean.log with no rotation or size cap, and the tool's purpose is long-lived TUI sessions whose screens redraw continuously, so logs realistically reach hundreds of MB over hours/days. Daemon amplification verified: main auto-routes `read` through the daemon when the socket is reachable (lines 203-218, 261-265); handle_daemon_request line 409 runs read_output_text inside the daemon process; run_daemon line 350 spawns a thread per connection, so concurrent manager polls each allocate the entire file simultaneously; start_session_in_daemon line 610 runs supervise_pty on a daemon thread, so daemon death ends every daemon-owned session's capture/forward threads. This is not excluded by the "log rotation is future work" carve-out: the defect is the O(file-size) read algorithm, fixed independently of rotation. Severity stays medium, not high: harm is gradual (minutes/hours sessions are fine; needs large accumulated logs), the default read path uses the smaller clean.log, and macOS rarely OOM-kills (it swaps/compresses), so the reliably reachable failure is multi-second per-poll latency and repeated full-file transient allocations in the durable daemon — a "large inputs" plausible-but-less-common condition per the guide.
verdict.fix: In read_output_text, replace fs::read_to_string with a bounded tail: open the file, fstat for length, seek backward from EOF reading fixed-size blocks (e.g. 64 KiB) and counting newlines until `tail` lines are found or a hard byte cap (e.g. a few MiB) is hit, then decode just that suffix with String::from_utf8_lossy (which also removes the UTF-8-strictness failure on raw.log) and return the last `tail` lines. read_screen_text can keep the simple path since screen.txt is rewritten per chunk and bounded by the vt100 grid size.

finding.title: Daemon-started sessions use the daemon's PATH/env, while `doctor` always diagnoses the client's environment
