finding.detail: supervise_pty resolves the program via path_with_local_bin(), which reads the PATH of the process it runs in — the daemon when sessions are daemon-owned — and CommandBuilder inherits the full daemon environment (src/main.rs:700-730). Meanwhile `doctor` is never routed through the daemon (daemon_request_for_command returns None, src/main.rs:274), so it inspects the CLIENT's PATH/env. Concrete scenario: the daemon was started from a plain Terminal session without nvm/homebrew init (or later via launchd); the user's iTerm shell has node and env vars (ANTHROPIC_*, custom PATH). `agent-bridge start dev --cmd "npm run dev"` fails with 'session stopped while starting: No such file or directory', or claude launches without the auth/env the user's shell has — yet `agent-bridge doctor` reports everything resolves fine, because it tested a different environment than the one that actually spawns the child.
finding.anchor: supervise_pty src/main.rs:702 (doctor never daemon-routed: daemon_request_for_command src/main.rs:274)
finding.fix: Include the client's PATH (or a chosen env subset) in DaemonRequest::Start and use it for resolution/spawn, and add a daemon-side doctor (or have doctor report that a reachable daemon's environment is what actually applies).

verdict.reasoning: Every prong checks out against the code and the pinned crate. (1) Start auto-routes to a reachable daemon (src/main.rs:203-218, 243-247) and DaemonRequest::Start (lines 160-164) carries only name/cwd/cmd — no PATH or env. (2) Daemon-owned sessions run supervise_pty on a thread inside the daemon process (start_session_in_daemon, lines 610-611), so path_with_local_bin() (line 702 → 1234, std::env::var("PATH")) reads the DAEMON's PATH. Verified in vendored portable-pty 0.8.1: CommandBuilder::new captures std::env::vars_os() of the current process (cmdbuilder.rs get_base_env, lines 72-95/209-212), and as_command() resolves the program via search_path() against the builder's PATH env, then env_clear() + builder envs (lines 449-489). Both resolution and child env are therefore the daemon's; client-shell-only exports (nvm/homebrew PATH, ANTHROPIC_*) never reach the child. (3) If the program is absent from the daemon PATH ∪ ~/.local/bin, search_path bails, supervise_pty errors at lines 732-735, the daemon thread marks the session Stopped (611-618), and wait_for_running_metadata (672-680) returns "session '…' stopped while starting: failed to spawn command in PTY" to the client. (Claim's quoted error text "No such file or directory" is slightly inaccurate — anyhow Display shows only the outermost context — but the start failure is real.) If found via force-prepended ~/.local/bin (e.g. claude), it launches with the daemon env, missing shell-only auth vars due to env_clear. (4) doctor is never daemon-routed (line 274) and diagnoses the CLIENT environment (lines 436-513: client path_with_local_bin at 447, client fs checks, login-shell probe), never detecting a reachable daemon — so it reports "executable_found: true / warnings: none" while daemon-routed start fails. (5) This is not excluded future work: docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md lists "preserve normal user environment and auth state" (line 211) and PATH-based resolution (lines 232-234) as required behavior, and its remaining-work list omits env propagation. Refutation attempts failed: envs match when the daemon is started from the user's configured shell (the common manual workflow — which is why this is medium, not high), but launchd/SSH/IDE daemon launches and post-launch env drift are plausible; --direct exists but is opt-in, debugging-labeled, and nothing in doctor or the error message points to it. Severity stays medium: real failure plus actively misleading diagnostics, but only under plausible-but-less-common env-divergent daemon conditions, with an (undiscoverable) workaround.
verdict.fix: Add the client environment to the start request and make doctor daemon-aware. Minimal version: (a) extend DaemonRequest::Start with `path: Option<String>` (client fills it with std::env::var("PATH")); thread it into start_session_in_daemon → supervise_pty so resolve_program_path and `command.env("PATH", …)` use the client-supplied PATH (falling back to the process PATH in direct mode) — optionally extend to a full env-var map for auth vars; (b) in doctor, attempt UnixStream::connect(socket_path()) and, if reachable, print a prominent warning that a running daemon will spawn sessions with the daemon's environment, not the one being diagnosed (or add a DaemonRequest::Doctor so the daemon reports its own PATH/resolution alongside the client's).

finding.title: Session exit is never detected when grandchildren hold the PTY slave: output-thread join hangs and metadata stays Running forever
finding.detail: After child.wait() returns, supervise_pty blocks on output_thread.join(), but the PTY master only sees EOF once ALL slave fds close. Run `start sh --cmd sh`, type `sleep 1000 &` then `exit`: the shell dies, sleep inherits the slave as stdin/stdout/stderr, capture_output blocks in read() and the join hangs indefinitely. mark_stopped never runs, so status reports status=Running (child_alive=false) forever — a manager agent polling for completion waits forever. The doc's advertised targets (shells, dev servers, test watchers) routinely background children, and daemonizing dev servers trigger this; in daemon mode the session thread is also permanently wedged.
finding.fix: Mark the session Stopped with the exit status immediately after child.wait() returns, and let the reader thread drain remaining output on its own (or join with a deadline) instead of gating mark_stopped on PTY EOF.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed against /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs and portable-pty 0.8.1 sources. In supervise_pty, after child.wait() returns (main.rs:760), the code does drop(pair.master) (762) then blocks on output_thread.join() (763), and mark_stopped only runs after supervise_pty returns (run_supervisor main.rs:694-697; daemon thread main.rs:610-619). The reader thread (capture_output, main.rs:783) sits in a plain blocking read() on a dup of the PTY master: portable-pty 0.8.1 try_clone_reader() and take_writer() both dup the master fd (unix.rs:314-326), so drop(pair.master) closes only one of three master fds and cannot force anything. A pty master read returns EOF/EIO only when the LAST slave fd closes. spawn_command gives the child the slave as fds 0/1/2 (setsid+TIOCSCTTY, parent-side slave dups closed; unix.rs:194-258), so `sh` running `sleep 1000 &` then `exit` leaves the background grandchild holding three slave fds; session-leader exit SIGHUPs only the foreground pgrp, so the background job survives (the classic "ssh hangs on logout" pty mechanism). child.wait() is std::process::Child::wait (portable-pty lib.rs:268-270) and reaps only the direct child, so it returns while the reader stays blocked and join() hangs for the grandchild's lifetime (indefinitely for daemonizing dev servers). Nothing reconciles state: status_text (main.rs:959-983) and list print metadata.status verbatim, so the session reports status=Running, child_alive=false, no exit_status, forever; a polling manager never sees Stopped, and in direct mode a re-start of the same name is refused (main.rs:537-545) because the wedged supervisor is alive. In daemon mode the session thread is permanently wedged (and the forward_input thread, which holds the FIFO O_RDWR and never EOFs, main.rs:809-830, leaks alongside it). Severity stays medium: normal flows (interact, then `stop`) work — stop_session_silent signals the supervisor/child and calls mark_stopped, clearing the stale state — and the failure needs the specific but plausible condition of the session child exiting on its own while a forked grandchild still holds the tty.
verdict.fix: In supervise_pty, persist the terminal state as soon as the child is reaped instead of gating it on PTY EOF: immediately after child.wait() returns, call mark_stopped(name, Some(format!("{exit_status:?}"))) (or restructure so run_supervisor/the daemon closure do it before joining), then join the output thread with a bounded deadline (e.g., poll JoinHandle::is_finished for up to ~2s to drain final output) or leave it detached in daemon mode. This keeps metadata accurate even when grandchildren hold the slave open.

finding.title: Any capture-thread write error (disk full) silently kills output capture and freezes the session as a zombie 'Running'
finding.detail: capture_output returns Err on the first failed raw/clean/screen write — e.g. the disk fills overnight — and the thread exits, but nothing observes it: supervise_pty discards the result with `let _ = output_thread.join()` (src/main.rs:763) and joins only after child.wait(). With no reader draining the PTY master, the child blocks forever on its next write to a full PTY buffer, so child.wait() never returns either. Observable result: status/list report Running and child_alive=true indefinitely, `screen` and `read` serve stale content, no error is logged anywhere, and the manager agent waits on a session that can never make progress until someone runs stop. A single transient screen.txt write failure also takes down raw/clean logging because all three share one loop.
finding.fix: On capture failure, log the error and terminate the child / mark the session stopped with the capture error as exit_status, and don't let a screen.txt write failure abort raw/clean logging.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code walk of /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) Trigger: in capture_output (768-806) every per-chunk operation propagates with `?` — raw_log.write_all (789), clean_log.write_all (795), and fs::write of screen.txt (801) all return Err(ENOSPC) on a full disk (the File::flush calls are no-ops and cannot fail, but the writes can). One loop serves all three sinks, so a screen.txt-only failure also kills raw/clean logging, as claimed. The tool's own unbounded raw/clean logs (rotation is listed future work) make disk-full plausible on long sessions. (2) Unobserved death: supervise_pty's main thread is blocked in child.wait() (760); `let _ = output_thread.join();` (763) runs only after the child exits and discards the Result anyway; the thread returns Err rather than panicking, so nothing surfaces in either direct mode (run_supervisor 694-697) or daemon mode (start_session_in_daemon 610-619). (3) Deadlock: pair.master stays open on supervise_pty's stack until line 762 and the input thread permanently holds the take_writer() handle, so the child gets no EOF/SIGHUP/EIO; the only drained path was the capture thread's dup'd reader. With nobody reading the master, the bounded macOS tty output queue fills and the child's blocking write(2) to the slave wedges forever, so child.wait() never returns and mark_stopped is never called. (4) Observable zombie: status_text/list_sessions_text (959-1030) report status=Running and child_alive=true because process_alive (1308) is just kill(pid,0) and a write-blocked process is alive; read/screen (915-949) serve stale files; send (870-879) accepts input the wedged child cannot consume. No watchdog exists; only user-initiated stop_session (1071-1102) recovers via SIGTERM/SIGKILL. Weaker sub-case: if the child writes little after the failure, there is no freeze, but all subsequent output is silently lost and the capture error swallowed when the child eventually exits. Severity stays medium per the guide: real silent failure plus stale 'Running' state, but only under plausible-but-less-common conditions (ENOSPC/EDQUOT/EIO), not normal everyday use.
verdict.fix: Two minimal changes in src/main.rs: (a) make capture_output resilient — on a raw/clean/screen write failure, record the error once (e.g., to metadata or an errors file) and keep reading the PTY (drop the data or retry per-sink) so the child can never block on an undrained master; handle the screen.txt fs::write failure independently so it cannot abort raw/clean logging. (b) Stop discarding the thread result: replace `let _ = output_thread.join()` with logic that captures the capture error and folds it into the exit_status passed to mark_stopped; alternatively, on unrecoverable capture failure have the supervisor kill the child so child.wait() returns and the session is marked Stopped with the capture error instead of staying Running forever.

finding.title: Daemon startup socket takeover is a check-connect-remove-bind TOCTOU that can unlink a live daemon's socket
finding.detail: run_daemon does exists -> connect-probe -> remove_file -> bind as four separate steps (lines 333-342). Two `agent-bridge daemon` invocations racing after a crash (stale socket present) both fail the connect probe, both decide the socket is stale; D1 removes and binds (now live), then D2 — which already passed its probe — removes D1's freshly bound socket file and binds its own. D1 keeps running and 'listening' on an unlinked inode forever: a silent zombie daemon process that no client can ever reach, while clients talk to D2. The probe can also hit D1 between bind and listen and read ECONNREFUSED, with the same takeover result. The daemon additionally never removes its socket on shutdown (exit(0) at line 388), guaranteeing every restart exercises this stale-socket path.
finding.fix: Guard daemon startup with an exclusive flock on a lock file (held for the daemon's lifetime) before removing/binding the socket, and unlink the socket on shutdown.

verdict.reasoning: Code matches the claim exactly. run_daemon (src/main.rs:333-342) performs exists -> connect-probe -> remove_file -> bind as four separate non-atomic steps with no flock/lockfile/O_EXCL guard anywhere in the binary (grep confirms). Precondition is guaranteed: the shutdown path calls std::process::exit(0) at line 388 without unlinking the socket (shutdown_sessions_for_daemon only stops sessions; the only socket unlink is the stale-cleanup at line 337; UnixListener Drop never unlinks and exit() skips Drop), so a stale socket file exists after every daemon stop or crash, making the racy branch the normal startup path. Failure interleaving verified step by step: two daemon starts D1/D2 both probe the stale socket via connect(2) -> ECONNREFUSED (AF_UNIX with no listener; the code itself relies on this semantic at line 283) -> both proceed as 'stale'; D1 unlinks and binds (live, prints 'listening' at 346); D2's fs::remove_file then succeeds on D1's freshly bound socket (unlink(2) ignores live bindings, man 7 unix), and D2 binds the freed path. D1 blocks forever in listener.incoming() (line 347) on the unlinked inode — accept(2) returns no error and can never receive a connection by path — a silent zombie that agent-bridge shutdown can never reach (clients and shutdown route to D2). All other interleavings fail cleanly because the losing remove_file gets NotFound and bails, so the claim identified the one harmful ordering correctly. The secondary bind-vs-listen window is also technically real (std UnixListener::bind issues bind(2) then listen(2) as separate syscalls; connect in between yields ECONNREFUSED) but is nanosecond-scale and not load-bearing. Impact is a leaked, unreachable daemon process with misleading 'listening' output; no data loss (the zombie owns no sessions), the winning daemon is fully functional, and the workaround is kill. Trigger needs two near-simultaneous daemon launches hitting a few-syscall window, so per the severity guide this is resource-hygiene/hardening: low.
verdict.fix: In run_daemon, before the probe/remove/bind sequence, open ~/.agent-bridge/daemon.lock and take an exclusive non-blocking flock (flock(fd, LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB), available on macOS and Linux via nix or libc); on EWOULDBLOCK bail with "daemon already running or starting". Keep the lock fd alive (e.g. leak it or store it in a struct living for the accept loop) so the lock is held for the daemon's lifetime — flock releases automatically on process death, covering crashes. Additionally, unlink the socket path before std::process::exit(0) in handle_daemon_stream's shutdown branch so clean restarts no longer depend on the stale-socket cleanup path.

finding.title: Daemon-mode error between spawn and wait leaks an unreaped child that becomes a permanent zombie and wedges stop/shutdown
finding.detail: In supervise_pty, any error after spawn_command but before child.wait() — e.g. load_metadata at line 738 hitting a torn metadata write from the polling client, or try_clone_reader/take_writer failing — returns early without ever waiting on the child. In direct mode the supervisor exits and init reaps; in the daemon the child stays a child of the daemon process forever. When it eventually exits it becomes a permanent zombie: kill(pid, 0) succeeds on zombies so process_alive=true, SIGTERM/SIGKILL are accepted but do nothing, and terminate_pid bails 'process did not exit after SIGTERM and SIGKILL' — `stop` fails forever for that session and `shutdown` (which bails if any Running session fails to stop) refuses to exit the daemon.
finding.fix: Guarantee reaping on every path after spawn (kill + child.wait() in an error/drop guard), and move the fallible metadata update before spawn_command.

verdict.reasoning: Core mechanism confirmed against code and portable-pty 0.8.1 source. In daemon mode the PTY child is a direct child of the daemon process: start_session_in_daemon (src/main.rs:610) runs supervise_pty on an in-daemon thread, and portable-pty's UnixSlavePty::spawn_command (unix.rs:297 -> unix.rs:194) is a plain std::process::Command::spawn() with setsid in pre_exec; the returned Box<dyn Child> is a bare std::process::Child (impl Child for std::process::Child, lib.rs:260), which has no Drop that kills or reaps, and the daemon has no SIGCHLD handling. Between spawn_command (main.rs:732-735) and child.wait() (main.rs:760) there are four fallible calls: load_metadata (738), save_metadata (743), try_clone_reader (745-748) and take_writer (749-752) — the dups fail under EMFILE (macOS default soft fd limit 256; ~6 fds per session), save under ENOSPC. Any `?` returns early without waiting; pair.master is dropped, SIGHUP kills the child (pre_exec restored SIG_DFL), and it becomes a permanent zombie of the daemon. kill(pid,0) succeeds on zombies, so process_alive (1308) stays true forever; terminate_pid (1104-1131) sends SIGTERM/SIGKILL (accepted no-ops on a zombie) and bails after ~4s — so if child_pid was recorded (the try_clone_reader/take_writer sub-case, since save_metadata at 743 ran first), `stop` of that session fails forever and status/list show child_alive=true misleadingly. Direct mode is indeed unaffected (supervisor exits, init reaps). However two parts of the claim are overstated: (1) the shutdown wedge is NOT the normal outcome — the daemon thread's mark_stopped (616) usually succeeds, leaving status Stopped (or Starting/unreadable if 738/743 failed), and shutdown_sessions_for_daemon (1046) only stops Running sessions, so it skips the session and exits; the wedge requires a second independent failure (mark_stopped also failing, e.g. sustained fd exhaustion) to leave status Running. (2) The example trigger is wrong: the polling client (wait_for_running_metadata, 665-684) only reads metadata and cannot cause a torn write at 738; that would need a racing duplicate start. Net: real unreaped-child/zombie leak for the daemon's lifetime with misleading child_alive and a failing stop on that session, under rare trigger conditions, with an easy workaround (daemon restart reparents the zombie to init, which reaps it). That is resource hygiene plus misleading-error UX: severity low, matching the claim's own rating.
verdict.fix: In supervise_pty, guarantee reaping on every post-spawn path: wrap the fallible section between spawn_command and child.wait() (load/save metadata, try_clone_reader, take_writer) so that on any Err it runs `let _ = child.kill(); let _ = child.wait();` before propagating (e.g. an inner closure/function whose Err arm reaps, or a small drop-guard holding the child). Additionally move the metadata load/update before spawn_command to shrink the window, and optionally have stop_session_silent skip terminate_pid for sessions already marked Stopped to avoid the 4s failing stop on stale pids.

finding.title: 5-second start timeout reports failure but leaves the detached supervisor running, contradicting itself
finding.detail: wait_for_running_metadata polls 50x100ms and bails with 'did not report running within 5 seconds' — but by then the setsid'd supervisor (or daemon thread) has already been spawned and is not cleaned up on this path. Under load or slow disk/binary resolution the session then transitions to Running moments later: the user was told start failed, yet `list` shows it Running and a retry of the same start either errors 'already running' or, if it lands while status is still Starting, re-initializes the files under the live supervisor (feeding the duplicate-spawn race). The timeout expiring mid-operation leaves orphaned in-flight state instead of converging to either success or a cleaned-up failure.
finding.fix: On timeout, either kill the spawned supervisor/thread before bailing or report 'still starting (pid N); check status' instead of a hard failure.

verdict.reasoning: All four claimed mechanics check out against src/main.rs. (1) wait_for_running_metadata (lines 665-684) polls 50x100ms and bails at line 683 with "did not report running within 5 seconds". (2) By then the detached worker already exists and is never cleaned up: in direct mode start_session_detached spawns the setsid'd supervisor at lines 573-575 and discards the Child handle (drop does not kill); the timeout error at line 577 just propagates to main with no kill/mark_stopped and no pid in the message. In daemon mode the thread spawned at line 610 keeps running after the timeout at line 621; the client only gets ok:false. (3) The supervisor side (run_supervisor 686-698 -> supervise_pty 738-743) has no deadline: it flips status to Running whenever openpty/spawn_command complete, even long after 5s (plausible under heavy load/swap, slow AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME filesystem, or Gatekeeper first-exec stalls), so list/status then show Running despite the reported failure. (4) A retry after the flip hits the guard at lines 538-544 and errors "already running"; a retry landing while status is still Starting passes that guard (it only checks Running) and initialize_session_files at line 547 unlinks/recreates the FIFO, truncates the logs, and overwrites metadata under the live supervisor, then spawns a second supervisor — the duplicate-spawn clobber. No code path reconciles the timeout report with the still-progressing start. Refutation attempts failed: Child drop never kills; the supervisor never self-aborts; the Stopped branch (672-680) only covers failure, not slow success. Severity stays low: the >5s trigger is a tail condition, the direct harm is a self-contradicting error with an easy workaround (status/list show the truth), and the destructive re-init follow-on additionally requires the separate Starting-guard race.
verdict.fix: On the timeout path, converge instead of contradicting: in wait_for_running_metadata's final branch (src/main.rs:683), load the metadata and either (a) terminate the in-flight start — call terminate_pid on supervisor_pid/child_pid (best-effort) and mark_stopped(name, Some("start timed out")) so the failure leaves a clean Stopped state — or (b) return a non-failure message like "session 'NAME' is still starting (supervisor pid N); check 'agent-bridge status NAME'". Additionally, extend the already-running guard at lines 538-544 (and the daemon variant at 595-603) to also bail when status == Starting with a live supervisor/child, closing the retry-clobber window.

finding.title: Daemon responses flatten error chains, hiding root causes and conflating missing session with corrupt state
finding.detail: handle_daemon_request serializes failures with error.to_string(), which for anyhow errors yields only the outermost context: a status/read of a nonexistent session returns just 'failed to read /Users/x/.agent-bridge/sessions/NAME/metadata.json' with the 'No such file or directory' cause stripped, indistinguishable from a permission problem or torn write; a send to a dead session returns 'failed to open input FIFO for NAME' without the ENXIO that explains the reader is gone. Since most commands auto-route through the daemon, this is the error UX the manager agent normally sees, and nothing anywhere says plainly 'no such session NAME'.
finding.fix: Serialize the full chain with format!("{error:#}") and map a missing metadata.json to an explicit 'no such session' error in load_metadata.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by code walk of /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) handle_daemon_request (lines 428-432) serializes failures as error.to_string() on an anyhow::Error (anyhow 1.0.102 in Cargo.lock); anyhow's Display renders only the outermost context, the source chain needs {:#}/{:?}. (2) Nonexistent session: Status/Read/Screen route to load_metadata (line 1176) or fs::read_to_string with context "failed to read <path>"; through the daemon the io NotFound cause is stripped, so the client prints only "Error: failed to read .../sessions/NAME/metadata.json" (main line 211 bails with the flat string). Grep confirms no "no such session" message exists anywhere, and load_metadata has no existence check, so NotFound is textually identical to EACCES or invalid-UTF-8 reads. (3) Information is genuinely lost relative to direct mode: with --direct or no daemon, the same anyhow error exits main() and is printed via Debug including "Caused by: No such file or directory (os error 2)" — so auto-routing through the daemon (default when the socket is reachable, main lines 203-219) strictly degrades the error. The daemon also never logs the request error (the {error:#} eprintln at line 352 only covers stream-level failures), so the chain is lost entirely. (4) The send/ENXIO example holds for owners killed without mark_stopped (metadata still Running, write_session_bytes line 878-882 opens the FIFO O_WRONLY|O_NONBLOCK and the "Device not configured" cause is stripped); cleanly-stopped sessions instead get a clear "session 'NAME' is not running", so that sub-case is narrower but the mechanism is correct. Severity stays low: error-path-only, message is true but incomplete, the path hints at the cause, and --direct is an easy workaround — matching the guide's "misleading-error UX with an easy workaround".
verdict.fix: In handle_daemon_request (src/main.rs line 431), serialize the full chain: error: Some(format!("{error:#}")). Additionally, in load_metadata (line 1176), map a missing metadata.json to an explicit error, e.g. match the io::ErrorKind::NotFound case (or check session_dir existence) and bail!("no such session '{name}'") so a typoed name is stated plainly in both modes.

finding.title: Malformed or unknown daemon requests get no response, so clients see only 'daemon closed connection without a response'
finding.detail: handle_daemon_stream bails on read/parse failure BEFORE writing any DaemonResponse (src/main.rs:370-378); the error goes to the daemon's stderr and the stream drops, so the client's read_line gets EOF and reports the generic 'daemon closed connection without a response' (src/main.rs:301-302). Concrete scenario (version skew): the user rebuilds/upgrades agent-bridge while an old daemon is still running; the new CLI sends a request variant or tag the old daemon's serde enum rejects, and every command fails with that unexplained message until the user guesses to restart the daemon. The same applies to the planned MCP adapter or any hand-written client sending slightly wrong JSON — the protocol gives them zero diagnostic. (The daemon's own startup liveness probe also triggers a spurious 'empty daemon request' stderr line via this path.)
finding.fix: On read/parse failure, write a DaemonResponse{ok:false, error: parse error text} (optionally with a protocol version) before closing the stream.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code walk. (1) handle_daemon_stream (src/main.rs:363-392) has three no-response exit paths before the first write of a DaemonResponse at line 381: read_line error (370-372), bail!("empty daemon request") on empty line (373-375), and serde_json::from_str parse failure (377-378). On any of these the error goes only to the daemon's stderr (run_daemon, line 352) and the stream drops. (2) The client's try_send_daemon_request then gets EOF: BufReader::read_line returns Ok(0) leaving the buffer empty, so it hits bail!("daemon closed connection without a response") (301-302). main() propagates this via `?` at line 205 — the direct-mode fallback only triggers on NotFound/ConnectionRefused connect errors (282-283), so a reachable-but-rejecting daemon makes the command hard-fail with that single unexplained line. (3) Version-skew scenario is concrete: DaemonRequest is internally tagged (#[serde(tag = "command")], lines 157-158) and serde rejects unknown variant tags, so a rebuilt CLI sending a new variant to a still-running old daemon fails to parse with zero client-visible diagnostic; the real error ("failed to parse daemon request: unknown variant ...") lands only on the daemon's stderr. Minor overstatement in the claim: "every command fails" only holds if existing variants' wire format changed — merely adding a variant breaks only the new command (no deny_unknown_fields, so added fields are ignored). (4) The spurious stderr line is also real: run_daemon's liveness probe (line 334) connects and drops without writing, so the running daemon's handler hits the empty-line bail and prints "daemon request failed: empty daemon request" whenever a second daemon start is attempted. Refutations considered and rejected: matched-version operation makes the parse path dead code (serde_json round-trips its own output), but the long-running-daemon + rebuild scenario is everyday use for this tool, and the probe noise is triggerable today; the daemon-side stderr log does not reach the client. Severity stays low per the guide: misleading-error UX with an easy workaround (restart/kill the daemon; the Shutdown variant predates any skew and still parses). No data loss and no failure in matched-version normal use.
verdict.fix: In handle_daemon_stream, on empty-line or parse failure, write a DaemonResponse { ok: false, output: String::new(), error: Some(format!("invalid daemon request: {err}")) } plus "\n" to the stream (and flush) before returning, so clients receive the actual parse error instead of EOF. Optionally treat a zero-byte read (pure EOF, e.g. the startup liveness probe) as a silent no-op instead of an error to eliminate the spurious "empty daemon request" stderr line.

finding.title: `send` text beginning with '-' is rejected by clap instead of being sent to the session
finding.detail: The Send `text` positional has no allow_hyphen_values (src/main.rs:62-70), so clap treats leading-dash text as an option. Concrete scenario: the manager agent drives a Python REPL and runs `agent-bridge send py "-1 + 2"`, or sends `--help`/`-v` to a CLI under test; clap exits code 2 with 'unexpected argument' and nothing reaches the session. The `--` escape works but an AI client has no reason to know it's required, and for a tool whose whole job is forwarding arbitrary text this is a recurring trap.
finding.fix: Add #[arg(allow_hyphen_values = true)] to the `text` positional (and document `--` for the keys list).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by walking the exact parse path against src/main.rs and the vendored clap_builder-4.6.0 source (Cargo.lock pins clap 4.6.1 / clap_builder 4.6.0).

Failure walk: (1) /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs:201 — `Cli::parse()` runs before any send logic or daemon routing, so argv hits clap first in both direct and daemon modes. (2) main.rs:62-70 — the `text` positional has no `#[arg]` attribute; grep confirms `allow_hyphen_values`, `allow_negative_numbers`, and `trailing_var_arg` appear nowhere in the file. (3) For `agent-bridge send py "-1 + 2"`: clap_builder parser.rs:693 (`is_new_arg`) only lets a hyphen-prefixed token be consumed as a positional value if `is_allow_hyphen_values_set()` (or negative-numbers, also unset), so the token takes the short-flag path; `parse_short_arg` finds no registered short flag and returns `NoMatchingArg`; parser.rs:270-281 builds `ClapError::unknown_argument`; error/mod.rs:233-248 + util/mod.rs:32 (`USAGE_CODE = 2`) make `Cli::parse()` call `process::exit(2)`. Nothing is written to input.fifo. (4) `send py --help` is intercepted by the auto-generated help flag and exits 0 printing agent-bridge's help; `send py -v` is an unknown argument (version is `-V` on the top-level command only, no propagate_version) and exits 2. In every case the text never reaches the session, exactly as claimed.

Why only low: the failure is loud and clean (exit code 2, no partial send, no state corruption), and clap 4.6 prints the workaround in the error itself — parser.rs:273-275 sets `suggested_trailing_arg = !trailing_values && cmd.has_positionals()` (true for `send`), and error/mod.rs:737 emits "to pass '-1 + 2' as a value, use '-- -1 + 2'". An AI client therefore self-corrects from stderr on the next attempt. Most everyday payloads don't begin with '-'. This fits the guide's "misleading-error UX with an easy workaround" tier, though it is a genuine trap for a tool whose purpose is forwarding arbitrary text.

Fix safety verified: in clap_builder 4.6.0 `parse_long_arg`, registered long flags are matched before the unknown-arg hyphen-value fallback (the `pos_counter` allow-hyphen branch at parser.rs:866-875 is the else-if after flag matching, and the parse_state short-circuit at parser.rs:776 only applies mid-consumption of that arg), so adding `allow_hyphen_values = true` to `text` does not break `--no-enter` or `--help` placed before/instead of text. Residual: sending the literal strings `--help` or `--no-enter` still requires `--` (inherent flag/value ambiguity), worth one line in the help text.
verdict.fix: In /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs add `#[arg(allow_hyphen_values = true)]` above the `text: String` field of `Commands::Send` (line 65-66). Optionally mention in the field's doc comment that literal `--help`/`--no-enter` text still needs the `--` escape (e.g. `agent-bridge send NAME -- --help`).

finding.title: Doc example `agent-bridge send claude "..." --enter` errors: the flag is --no-enter
finding.detail: docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md line 375 ('Command Surface — Initial service-era CLI') shows `agent-bridge send claude "Review the failing tests." --enter`. Verified: this exits 2 with "unexpected argument '--enter' found". The CLI only defines --no-enter (Enter is appended by default), so the one documented send example in the command-surface block fails if pasted, while every other command in that block parses as written.
finding.fix: Update the doc example to drop --enter (default already presses Enter) and document --no-enter instead.

verdict.reasoning: docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md:375, inside the "Command Surface — Initial service-era CLI" block (lines 371-382, distinct from the separate "Possible future commands" block at 384-392), shows `agent-bridge send claude "Review the failing tests." --enter`. The actual CLI (src/main.rs:62-70) defines Send with positionals `name`/`text` and only `#[arg(long)] no_enter: bool`; clap 4.6.1 derive generates solely `--no-enter`, with no alias, no infer_long_args, no allow_hyphen_values on `text`, and no ignore_errors/external-subcommand settings (the only top-level attribute is `#[command(author, version, about)]`). Therefore `--enter` is an unknown argument and clap 4 rejects it with "unexpected argument '--enter' found" and exit code 2. The doc's own Phase 2 scope (line 289, "allow `send --no-enter`") and the implementation (send_input_silent, lines 839-841, appends \r unless no_enter) confirm Enter is the default and `--no-enter` is the real flag, so this is a doc bug, not an unimplemented roadmap feature. Cross-checking every other command in the block (daemon; doctor --cmd --cwd; start --cwd --cmd; keys; screen --tail; read --tail; status; list; stop) against the Commands enum shows they all parse as written — only the send example fails. Severity is low: documentation-only error with a trivial workaround, and clap's error output even suggests the similar `--no-enter` flag.
verdict.fix: In docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md line 375, drop `--enter` so the example reads `agent-bridge send claude "Review the failing tests."` (Enter is appended by default), and optionally add a second example showing `--no-enter` for suppressing it.

finding.title: README quickstart never exercises the daemon it starts: AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME on the daemon line only
finding.detail: README.md line 20 starts the daemon under AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME=/private/tmp/agent-bridge-test, but the following client commands (start/send/read/.../shutdown, lines 21-29) use the default home. Since the socket path derives from bridge_root, those clients look for ~/.agent-bridge/agent-bridge.sock, never find the test daemon, and silently run in direct mode under ~/.agent-bridge; the final `shutdown` prints 'daemon not running' while the test daemon keeps running. The block therefore contradicts the adjacent claim 'When a daemon is reachable, normal session commands route through its Unix socket' — a user following it verbatim tests the supervisor path while believing they tested the daemon.
finding.fix: Use one consistent AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME (or none) across the entire README command block.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/README.md (lines 17-30, 44-45) and src/main.rs. (1) bridge_root() (main.rs:1222-1229) reads AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME from the current process env, else ~/.agent-bridge; socket_path() (1218-1220) is bridge_root()/agent-bridge.sock, and grep confirms this is the ONLY socket-discovery mechanism (client caller at 279, daemon at 332). (2) README line 20's `AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME=/private/tmp/agent-bridge-test ./target/debug/agent-bridge daemon` is a one-command env prefix, so run_daemon (328-342) binds /private/tmp/agent-bridge-test/agent-bridge.sock; the env var does not persist to lines 21-29. (3) Those client commands therefore connect to ~/.agent-bridge/agent-bridge.sock; try_send_daemon_request (278-288) maps NotFound/ConnectionRefused to Ok(None) with no message, and main (203-238) silently falls through to direct-mode handlers — sessions are created via the supervisor path under ~/.agent-bridge/sessions, never touching the test daemon. (4) Line 29's `shutdown` dispatches shutdown_daemon_direct() (309-326), which on None prints "daemon not running" (line 322) and exits Ok while the test daemon keeps running. This contradicts README lines 44-45 ("When a daemon is reachable, normal session commands route through its Unix socket"): a user following the block verbatim exercises only the direct/supervisor path while believing they tested the daemon, and leaks a daemon process. Attempts to refute failed: there is no alternate socket discovery, no env propagation, and even reading the section as a command reference rather than a quickstart, the start→stop→shutdown ordering invites sequential execution and the daemon line is the only one with a different home. The code itself behaves as designed, so this is a documentation defect; severity low per the guide (misleading walkthrough with an easy workaround).
verdict.fix: In README.md, use one consistent home across the whole command block: either drop AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME from line 20, or add a preceding `export AGENT_BRIDGE_HOME=/private/tmp/agent-bridge-test` so the daemon and all client commands (start/send/read/.../shutdown) share the same socket and session root. Also note that `agent-bridge daemon` runs in the foreground, so it needs a separate terminal or `&` for the subsequent commands to be runnable.

finding.title: start reports failure for fast-exiting commands even when they succeed (exit code 0)
finding.detail: wait_for_running_metadata first sleeps 100ms; any command that finishes before the first poll is seen as Stopped and start bails. Verified: `agent-bridge start hi --cmd "echo hello"` exits 1 with "session 'hi' stopped while starting: ExitStatus { code: 0, signal: None }" even though clean.log captured 'hello'. The verified-so-far claim '`claude --version` works under the PTY' (Phase-0 acceptance) only holds because node startup exceeds 100ms — success of the documented acceptance test is timing-dependent, and any sub-100ms command yields a misleading error despite correct capture.
finding.fix: When the session reaches Stopped during the start wait with exit code 0, report success (return the metadata) instead of bailing.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code trace in /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. wait_for_running_metadata (lines 665-684) sleeps 100ms BEFORE its first load_metadata, then polls on a 100ms grid, and at line 672-679 bails on status==Stopped unconditionally — it never inspects whether the recorded exit status indicates success. For a sub-100ms command like `echo hello`, the supervisor (run_supervisor 686 -> supervise_pty 700) writes Running (738-743), child.wait() (760) returns within milliseconds, and mark_stopped (695 -> 1168-1174) writes Stopped with exit_status = format!("{exit_status:?}") (line 765) — portable-pty 0.8.1's ExitStatus {code: u32, signal: Option<String>} with derived Debug yields exactly "ExitStatus { code: 0, signal: None }". So the first poll at t=100ms sees Stopped and start bails; main() -> Result<()> (line 200) turns that into exit code 1 with the misleading message "session ... stopped while starting: ExitStatus { code: 0, signal: None }", even though capture_output (782-806) already drained the command's output into clean.log. The only escape is a poll randomly landing inside the few-ms Running window, so the failure is deterministic in practice. Both modes are affected identically: direct mode calls wait_for_running_metadata at line 577 and daemon mode at line 621 (daemon error propagates to CLI exit 1 via line 211). Severity stays low per the guide: it is misleading-error UX (wrong exit code + factually wrong "stopped while starting" message) with an easy workaround — the message itself contains code: 0, logs/status are correct, and the tool's core use case (long-running interactive programs) is unaffected; the documented `claude --version` acceptance only passes because node startup exceeds 100ms.
verdict.fix: Record the exit result in structured form and treat a clean fast exit as success. Concretely: in supervise_pty, capture portable_pty::ExitStatus before stringifying (it has exit_code()/success()) and pass it to mark_stopped so SessionMetadata gains e.g. exit_code: Option<u32> alongside the display string; then in wait_for_running_metadata (src/main.rs:672), when status == Stopped and exit_code == Some(0), return Ok(metadata) (callers can print "session ran to completion" instead of "started"), bailing only on nonzero/signal exits. Optionally also poll immediately and at a finer interval (e.g. 10-20ms) to narrow the window, but the exit-code check is the actual fix.

finding.title: Restarting a session name silently erases its previous raw/clean logs and screen
finding.detail: initialize_session_files uses File::create, which truncates raw.log, clean.log, and screen.txt whenever start reuses an existing name. A user who stops 'claude-main' on Friday and starts it again Monday loses the entire prior transcript, although the docs present raw.log as 'exact PTY bytes for replay/debugging' and Phase-3 acceptance expects restarts not to corrupt existing logs; neither README nor the design doc mentions truncation anywhere. Undocumented destruction of the artifact the docs tell users to rely on for replay.
finding.fix: Either document that start truncates per-session logs, or preserve history by rotating (rename with timestamp) or opening the logs in append mode.

verdict.reasoning: Mechanism verified: initialize_session_files (src/main.rs:647-649) uses File::create, which truncates, on raw.log, clean.log, and screen.txt; both start paths call it (line 547 direct, line 605 daemon). The reuse guard (lines 537-545, 595-603) only rejects names whose prior session is Running with a live pid, so a Stopped session name passes through and its logs are silently zeroed — the stop-Friday/start-Monday scenario is real. No rotation/archival exists anywhere; capture_output (lines 769-778) appends only within a run. Docs verified: docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md:118 designates raw.log as "exact PTY bytes for replay/debugging", Phase 5 plans replay/debug tooling on it, and neither README nor design doc mentions truncation. Aggravator: truncation runs before parse_command (line 654) and before supervisor spawn, so even a failed start (bad --cmd quoting, spawn error) destroys the prior logs without starting anything. One correction to the claim: the Phase-3 acceptance item "daemon restart does not corrupt existing logs" (doc line 324) is about restarting the daemon process, which never calls initialize_session_files — the code does not violate that criterion; the finding rests solely on the undocumented destruction of the documented replay artifact. Severity stays low: only historical logs of an already-stopped run are lost, the live session works, fresh-logs-per-run is a defensible (if unstated) semantic, and easy workarounds exist (start under a new name or copy logs first).
verdict.fix: In initialize_session_files, before the File::create calls, rename any existing non-empty raw.log/clean.log to timestamped siblings (e.g. raw.log.<unix_ts>) or open them with OpenOptions::append, and document the chosen behavior in README/design doc. Additionally, move parse_command validation ahead of the truncation so a start that fails to parse or spawn does not destroy prior logs.

finding.title: send immediately after start can hit the FIFO before any reader exists (ENXIO window): Running is saved before forward_input opens the FIFO
finding.detail: supervise_pty saves status=Running (src/main.rs:743) BEFORE the input-forwarder thread is spawned and opens the FIFO (src/main.rs:758/810). A send issued immediately after start observes status==Running, opens input.fifo O_WRONLY|O_NONBLOCK, and fails with ENXIO ('failed to open input FIFO') because no reader has the FIFO open yet. The same confusing ENXIO also appears whenever the owner died leaving stale Running metadata, making it ambiguous whether the session is starting or dead.
finding.fix: Save status=Running only after the forwarder thread holds the FIFO open, or retry ENXIO briefly in write_session_bytes.

verdict.reasoning: The ordering is exactly as claimed. supervise_pty persists status=Running at src/main.rs:740-743, then spawns the forwarder thread at :757-758, and the FIFO's first read-side open (O_RDWR) happens inside that thread at :809-814. The FIFO is freshly mkfifo'd per start (initialize_session_files :641-645 removes any old FIFO), so no prior handle can count as a reader. write_session_bytes (:870-888) gates only on metadata status==Running and opens the FIFO O_WRONLY|O_NONBLOCK (:878-882); per POSIX/macOS open(2), that fails with ENXIO when no reader exists, surfacing as "failed to open input FIFO". Both modes share the window (daemon Send at :398-404 calls the same write_session_bytes; start_session_in_daemon runs the same supervise_pty). Nothing synchronizes the metadata write with the FIFO open. However, the window is microseconds wide (two dups + two thread spawns + open), and both start paths block in wait_for_running_metadata (:577, :621) polling at 100ms granularity before returning, after which the send is a separate process launch — so hitting it requires multi-millisecond starvation of a runnable thread (heavy load). Consequence is one transient, misleading error; an immediate retry succeeds. The stale-Running/ENXIO half of the claim is also factually true (write_session_bytes does no liveness check, unlike start_session_detached :536-544), but that symptom is downstream of the explicitly excluded roadmap item (metadata recovery after owner/daemon death), so the finding rests on the startup race, which fits the low bucket: misleading-error UX with an easy workaround, plus a hardening gap.
verdict.fix: In supervise_pty, open the input FIFO O_RDWR before save_metadata(Running) (move the open out of forward_input: open the File at ~line 738, then pass it to the forwarder thread), so a reader provably exists whenever Running is visible. Optionally also retry ENXIO in write_session_bytes a few times with short sleeps and emit a clearer error ("no process is reading the session's input — session may still be starting or its owner died").

finding.title: clean.log silently deletes tabs (and every C0 control except \n), corrupting `read` output
finding.detail: strip_ansi_escapes::strip's vte Performer only forwards b'\n' from execute(); every other C0 control byte -- \t, lone \r, \x08 -- is silently dropped (verified in strip-ansi-escapes 0.2 source: `fn execute(&mut self, byte: u8) { if byte == b'\n' { ... } }`). Concrete failures: (1) any tab-containing output (cat-ing a Makefile inside a session, go test/gofmt output, TSV data, a REPL printing 'a\tb') reaches clean.log with the tabs deleted, so `agent-bridge read` shows 'ab' -- the manager AI sees silently corrupted file content and will reproduce the corruption if it copies it back; (2) lone-CR progress redraws (pip/npm/cargo spinners) lose their only line separator, fusing hundreds of overwritten frames into one mega-line, which breaks `--tail` line counting. This is distinct from the already-found chunk-boundary escape-splitting finding: it happens on every chunk, by the crate's design, with no chunking involved. The only workaround, `read --raw`, fails the stated purpose (it is full of ANSI noise) and is separately broken on non-UTF-8.
finding.fix:
Stop relying on strip_ansi_escapes for the clean log. Use a small vte-based stripper whose execute() preserves 	 and emits 
 for both 
 and bare  (collapsing 
 to one newline), or post-process chunks to translate lone  to 
 before stripping. (Pairs naturally with the already-planned fix to keep one stateful parser across chunks.)

verdict.reasoning:
Every load-bearing fact checks out against pinned sources. (1) src/main.rs:794-798: capture_output writes strip_ansi_escapes::strip(chunk) to clean.log; grep confirms this is the only clean.log writer for both direct and daemon modes. (2) Vendored strip-ansi-escapes 0.2.1 (Cargo.lock-pinned; ~/.cargo/registry/.../strip-ansi-escapes-0.2.1/src/lib.rs:165-170) has exactly the quoted Performer: execute() writes only for b'
'; all other C0 bytes are dropped. (3) vte 0.14.1 lib.rs:190 (Ground state) routes 0x00..=0x17 | 0x19 | 0x1C..=0x1F — including 	 (0x09),  (0x0D), \x08 — to execute(), never print(); the batch ground-dispatch path (line 725) does the same, so no path preserves tabs. (4) Refutation attempt via termios fails: portable-pty 0.8.1 unix.rs:32 calls libc::openpty with default termios; macOS defaults are opost onlcr -oxtabs, so tabs are NOT expanded and arrive as raw 0x09, while child 
 arrives as 
 (ONLCR) and survives as 
 (the  is dropped harmlessly), which is exactly why the bug is silent and content-dependent. Concrete walk-through: child prints "a	b
" -> master receives "a	b
" -> strip emits "ab
" -> clean.log corrupted; read_output_text (main.rs:915-930) serves clean.log by default, so the manager AI sees "ab". Lone-CR progress frames lose every separator and fuse into one line, and --tail counts via lines() (main.rs:921-922), so hundreds of frames count as one line. Mitigations exist but are weak: raw.log is intact but ANSI-laden (and read --raw uses fs::read_to_string at main.rs:919, which fails on non-UTF-8), and screen.txt (vt100) renders tabs/CR correctly but only shows the current viewport. Severity stays medium: silent wrong output of the primary read command under common-but-content-dependent conditions (tab-containing output, CR spinners), with correct data still preserved in raw.log/screen.txt and no crash or state loss.
verdict.fix:
Replace strip_ansi_escapes in capture_output (src/main.rs:794) with a small custom vte::Perform implementation kept as one persistent parser across read chunks (this also fixes the chunk-boundary escape-splitting issue). In its execute(): write the byte for b'	' and b'
'; for b'' emit a newline but suppress a newline for an immediately following b'
' (i.e., collapse 
 to one 
, turn lone  into 
); ignore the rest. vte 0.14.1 is already in the dependency tree via strip-ansi-escapes, so this adds no new dependency.

finding.title: Stale 'Running' metadata plus PID reuse falsely blocks `start` and falsifies status/list after any unclean shutdown
finding.detail: If the session owner dies without running mark_stopped (supervisor SIGKILLed, daemon crash, power loss), metadata.json keeps status=Running with the old pids forever. The start gate then trusts a bare kill(pid,0) liveness probe: start_session_detached src/main.rs:537-545 (and start_session_in_daemon src/main.rs:595-603) bails "session '<name>' is already running" as soon as the recorded supervisor/child pid has been recycled by ANY unrelated process -- routine on macOS where pids wrap at 99999 within days of normal use. Because process_alive (src/main.rs:1308-1314) also maps EPERM to alive, a pid recycled by a root-owned daemon blocks the name indefinitely. Result: a core command (`start`) refuses to work for that session name even though nothing is running, and status_text/list_sessions_text (src/main.rs:962-967, 1016-1018) report supervisor_alive/child_alive=true for an impostor process, misleading the operator. The only built-in remedy is `stop`, which the already-found stop finding shows SIGKILLs the innocent recycled pid and its process group -- so the safe path out is hand-editing metadata.json. This is the read-side/staleness half of the persisted-pid problem; the existing finding covers only stop's blind signaling.
finding.fix: Persist a process-identity token next to each pid (e.g., process start time via proc_pidinfo/PROC_PIDTBSDINFO on macOS, /proc/<pid>/stat starttime on Linux, or a per-session UUID exported into the supervisor's environment) and require it to match before treating the pid as the session; treat EPERM as not-ours in the start gate. When the identity check fails, reconcile metadata to Stopped instead of refusing to start.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by walking the failure path in /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) Stale state: status=Stopped is only ever written by mark_stopped (line 1168), reached from run_supervisor (695-696), the daemon session thread (616), or stop_session_silent (1099). No signal handlers and no reconciliation in start/status/list exist, so SIGKILL of the supervisor, daemon crash, or power loss leaves metadata.json at status=Running with stale pids forever. (2) Identity-blind gate: start_session_detached (537-545) bails "already running" when status==Running && process_alive(supervisor_pid); start_session_in_daemon (595-603) does the same on child_pid. process_alive (1308-1314) is kill(pid,0) with Ok=>true AND EPERM=>true, so any unrelated process that recycled the pid — including root-owned daemons we cannot signal — counts as the session. macOS xnu PID_MAX is 99999 with sequential wraparound, so reuse is plausible on a busy dev machine and especially after a power-loss reboot when low pids are re-held by long-lived system daemons. (3) status_text (962-967) and list_sessions_text (1016-1018) then report supervisor_alive/child_alive=true for the impostor alongside the stale Running status. (4) No safe built-in remedy: stop_session_silent (1078-1102) SIGTERM/SIGKILLs the recycled pid AND its process group via signal_pid_and_group (1133-1150); same-user impostor → innocent process killed (the separate stop finding); root-owned impostor → EPERM from both kill targets makes signal_pid_and_group bail, stop_session_silent bails at 1096 before mark_stopped (1099), so the name stays blocked until metadata.json is hand-edited. Refutation attempts failed: the gate does self-heal when the pid is simply dead (probe false → initialize_session_files resets), so the bug is confined to the pid-reuse window — exactly what the claim asserts; and the roadmap exclusion "metadata recovery on daemon restart" does not cover this, because the failure occurs in pure direct mode with no daemon involved and stems from the identity-blind probe in shipped code. One minor scope note: a daemon-started session (supervisor_pid=None) checked by the direct-mode gate is not blocked (is_some_and on None is false), but both anchored combinations behave as claimed. Severity stays medium per the guide ("crashes leaving stale state"): it needs an unclean shutdown plus a pid collision at probe time, but when it hits, start is falsely refused (indefinitely if the recycled pid is a long-lived/root process), status and list actively mislead, and the only non-destructive recovery is manual metadata editing.
verdict.fix: When recording supervisor_pid/child_pid, also persist a process-identity token — on macOS the process start time from proc_pidinfo(pid, PROC_PIDTBSDINFO) (pbi_start_tvsec/usec), on Linux the starttime field of /proc/<pid>/stat. In process_alive-based checks (start gates at src/main.rs:537-545 and 595-603, status_text, list_sessions_text, stop_session_silent), treat the pid as the session only if both the pid is alive AND the stored start time matches; treat EPERM as not-ours. On identity mismatch, reconcile metadata to Stopped (e.g., exit_status "lost: owner died") instead of bailing, so start proceeds and stop never signals a recycled pid.

finding.title: Client has no socket timeouts: every CLI command hangs forever when the daemon accepts but never replies
finding.detail: try_send_daemon_request never calls set_read_timeout/set_write_timeout (grep confirms zero timeouts in the whole binary). After a successful connect it blocks in read_line at src/main.rs:296-300 indefinitely. Concrete scenario requiring no malice: the docs/README run the daemon in a foreground terminal; the user suspends that terminal job with ctrl-Z (SIGSTOP) or the terminal app stops scheduling it. The kernel still completes new connections via the listen backlog and buffers the request write, so every subsequent agent-bridge command (start/send/read/screen/status/list/stop) hangs silently forever -- no error, no fallback to direct mode. For the primary use case (a manager AI shelling out to this CLI), one wedged daemon stalls the whole agent workflow with no diagnostic. This is the client-side counterpart, in a different process and code path, of the already-found daemon-side missing read timeout (that finding is about DoS-ing the daemon, not about the CLI hanging).
finding.fix: After connect, set_write_timeout and set_read_timeout on the UnixStream (e.g., 2s for control commands, ~10s for start/stop/shutdown which legitimately block longer), and map timeout errors to a clear 'daemon is unresponsive (pid file/socket: ...); retry with --direct or restart the daemon' message instead of hanging.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by code inspection of /Users/chadpeppers/Projects/agent-bridge/src/main.rs. (1) Grep shows zero set_read_timeout/set_write_timeout calls in the binary; the only Duration uses are thread::sleep. (2) main() (lines 200-219) routes every operational command (Start/Send/Keys/Read/Screen/Status/List/Stop/Shutdown via daemon_request_for_command, lines 241-276) through try_send_daemon_request unless --direct is given. (3) In try_send_daemon_request (lines 278-307), direct-mode fallback (Ok(None)) happens ONLY on connect-time NotFound or ConnectionRefused (lines 282-283). If the daemon process is SIGSTOPped (e.g., ctrl-Z on the foreground `agent-bridge daemon` job -- the only supported way to run it, since run_daemon never daemonizes), the kernel still completes new AF_UNIX connections into the listen backlog (std binds with backlog 128, line 342; SIGSTOP does not close fds), so connect succeeds and fallback is unreachable. (4) The small JSON request fits the kernel socket buffer, so the write (lines 290-294) succeeds. (5) read_line on the blocking stream (lines 296-300) then waits for data or EOF; a stopped daemon sends neither, so the CLI hangs silently and indefinitely with no output. There is no pid liveness check, watchdog, or signal handler to break the hang. Daemon-side handlers are all bounded (e.g., terminate_pid caps at ~4s), so a healthy daemon always replies -- but that is exactly why the failure needs and matches the suspended-daemon scenario. Backlog exhaustion would yield ECONNREFUSED-fallback only after 128 already-hung invocations, which a serial manager agent never reaches. Severity stays medium: normal use (daemon healthy or absent) works and --direct is a workaround, but the silent indefinite hang of every command with no diagnostic stalls the primary unattended-agent workflow under a plausible-but-less-common condition.
verdict.fix: In try_send_daemon_request, after UnixStream::connect succeeds, call stream.set_write_timeout(Some(...)) and stream.set_read_timeout(Some(...)) -- e.g., 2s for Send/Keys/Read/Screen/Status/List and ~15s for Start/Stop/Shutdown (stop legitimately takes up to ~4s of bounded polling daemon-side). On ErrorKind::WouldBlock/TimedOut, return a clear error such as "daemon at <socket path> accepted the connection but did not respond; it may be suspended or wedged -- resume/restart it or rerun with --direct" instead of propagating a bare IO error or hanging.

finding.title: doctor ships hardcoded author-machine heuristics that emit false warnings on every other install layout
finding.detail: doctor's warning logic encodes one specific machine's history: it warns whenever the resolved claude path is not ~/.local/bin/claude ("known-good local Claude path was ~/.local/bin/claude in this environment; resolved path differs", src/main.rs:475-477) and flags /opt/homebrew/bin/claude based on a past local incident (src/main.rs:469-474). On any standard installation where claude legitimately lives elsewhere (npm -g at /usr/local/bin/claude, a Homebrew install, or the planned Linux target where /opt/homebrew never exists but ~/.local/bin may not be used), a perfectly working setup is reported with warnings every single run. Since doctor's whole job is to be the trustworthy diagnostic -- and its consumer is often the manager AI deciding whether the environment is healthy -- persistent spurious warnings cause wrong conclusions and wasted debugging, and train users to ignore the warnings section entirely. Note doctor also already runs `claude --version` through the resolved path (src/main.rs:484-491), which provides the actual health signal these heuristics try to guess at.
finding.fix: Drop the hardcoded path comparisons; derive health from observed behavior doctor already collects (did `<resolved> --version` succeed, does login-shell resolution disagree with bridge resolution). If the historical /opt/homebrew note is worth keeping, demote it to an informational line printed only when that exact path is resolved AND the version probe fails.

verdict.reasoning: Verified at src/main.rs:468-477. doctor (default --cmd claude, line 116) pushes a warning whenever resolved_program contains "/opt/homebrew/bin/claude" (469-474), based solely on the path string — docs/AGENT-BRIDGE.md:87-91 shows this encodes a one-time snapshot of the author's machine where the brew binary happened to be a stale 2.1.81 — and pushes a second warning whenever the command's basename is "claude" but the resolved path does not contain "/.local/bin/claude" (475-477). command_is_claude (1266) is a bare basename check and resolve_program_path (1254) returns the first PATH hit after path_with_local_bin (1231) prepends ~/.local/bin, so any legitimate install layout other than ~/.local/bin/claude (npm -g at /usr/local/bin, Homebrew on Apple Silicon, Linux /usr/bin) triggers one or both warnings on every doctor run, even when the very same doctor invocation proves health via the `<resolved> --version` probe (486-491) and login-shell probe (495-500). The warnings are unconditional, unsuppressable, and printed in the warnings: section (507-510) that the manager-AI consumer treats as the health verdict. No mechanism refutes the scenario: the design doc mandates the ~/.local/bin-first PATH ordering (correctly implemented) but never specifies path-equality warnings; the "in this environment" hedge is simply false on other environments; and the /opt/homebrew message asserts a defect about whatever binary currently occupies that path regardless of its actual version. Severity stays low: doctor's exit code is unaffected, core commands work, and the genuine signal (probe output) is printed alongside, so this is misleading diagnostic UX with an easy workaround rather than a functional failure.
verdict.fix: Delete the two hardcoded path heuristics at src/main.rs:469-477 and derive warnings from data doctor already collects: warn if the `<resolved_program> --version` probe fails or emits nothing, and warn if the login-shell `command -v claude` output (already captured at 495-500, just needs parsing instead of only printing) resolves to a different path than resolved_program. If the historical /opt/homebrew incident is worth preserving, print it as an informational note only when that exact path resolved AND the version probe failed.

finding.title: Daemon socket is briefly group/world-connectable: chmod 0600 happens only after bind
finding.detail: UnixListener::bind creates the socket with 0777 & ~umask (typically 0755) inside the 0755 ~/.agent-bridge directory; fs::set_permissions(0600) runs afterwards, leaving a window in which another local user can connect (macOS does enforce write permission on the socket inode at connect time, so after chmod it's safe — but not before). A connection in that window can issue a Start request, i.e. execute an arbitrary command as the daemon user. The window is milliseconds and only at daemon startup, hence low, but it is free to eliminate.
finding.fix: Set umask(0077) (or chmod the parent dir to 0700, which create_dir_all should do anyway) before binding so the socket is never born permissive.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim is internally inconsistent and fails against real macOS semantics. Code path: src/main.rs:341-342 `UnixListener::bind(&path)` then src/main.rs:343-344 `fs::set_permissions(0o600)`. Grep confirms the binary never calls umask, so the socket inherits the process umask (default 022 on macOS).

XNU `unp_bind` creates the socket node with `va_mode = ACCESSPERMS & ~fd_cmask` = `0777 & ~umask`, so under the default umask 022 the socket is born 0755 — exactly the "typical 0755" the claim cites. That mode is owner=rwx, group=r-x, other=r-x: the WRITE bit is set only for the owner.

The claim's own load-bearing premise is correct: macOS enforces write permission at connect. XNU `unp_connect` performs `vnode_authorize(vp, NULL, KAUTH_VNODE_WRITE_DATA, ctx)` (matching FreeBSD's historical `VOP_ACCESS(vp, VWRITE)` and Linux unix(7), which also require write to connect). Given a 0755 socket, that check grants connect only to the owner; any other local user's `UnixStream::connect` during the window between line 342 and line 343 is denied EACCES and never reaches `handle_daemon_stream`/the `Start` request.

The two premises the claim states (mode 0755 + write-enforced-at-connect) directly forbid its conclusion (a non-owner can connect). Moreover, for the purpose of connect permission the chmod is a no-op under the default umask: 0755 and 0600 both grant write to the owner only, so the connectable principal set is {owner} both before and after line 343 — there is no instant of broader access, i.e. no exploitable window. The chmod only strips group/other read+search bits, which are irrelevant to connect authorization.

A real window would require a non-default permissive umask leaving the other-write bit set (umask 000 or 020) or the group-write bit set (umask 002) plus an attacker sharing the owner's supplementary group. That contradicts the claim's stated "typical 0755" case and is not the macOS default, so the everyday failure scenario described does not occur.

The single fact this hinges on is whether macOS checks read vs write at connect: it checks write (KAUTH_VNODE_WRITE_DATA), which the claim itself concedes — and that fact is precisely what makes 0755 non-connectable by non-owners.
verdict.fix: No fix required; the claimed window does not exist under the conditions described. The proposed hardening (umask(0077) or chmod the parent dir to 0700 before bind) is harmless defense-in-depth and would matter only if the daemon is ever run under an unusual permissive umask (000/020, or 002 with a shared group), but that is not the typical/default case and is not a confirmed bug.
